The Public Debt of the Colony at 31st December, 1960, consisted of £1,000,000 of 3% Debenture Loan, 1967–72, £400,000 of 3½% Debenture Loan, 1970–75, and £250,000 of 5% Debenture Loan, 1967–72. In addition there was a balance of £112,500 outstanding on an interest-free loan of £250,000 from Colonial Development and Welfare funds.
Currency

Gibraltar Government notes and United Kingdom silver and copper coins are legal tender in the Colony. Notes to the value of £1,317,447 were in circulation at 31st December 1960. The issue is governed by the Currency Note Ordinance and is of the following denominations : £5, £1, 10s.
Development Plans

Three hotels are under construction as well as new Customs, Police and Immigration buildings at the Frontier. With the assistance of Colonial Development and Welfare Funds, work is proceeding on about 400 new flats, a 10-acre rain water catchment and a road to Catalan Bay village.

Work commenced on the construction of three Homes for the Aged and the John Mackintosh Hall, a cultural and educational centre, the cost of which is being met from funds provided under the will of the late John Mackintosh.
Education

There are eight secondary and fourteen primary and infants' schools conducted by the Department of Education, with a total of 4,034 pupils at 31st December 1960. In addition there are three unaided schools with an enrolment of 575. Government expenditure on education in 1960 amounted to £137,826 compared with £9,863 in 1935.
Approximately 40 students of both sexes are undergoing courses of higher education in the United Kingdom.
Libraries and Museums

A lending library is maintained by the British Council. At the Gibraltar Garrison Library, established in 1793, an extensive reference section and a well-supplied lending library are available to members. The Museum contains exhibits of historical interest connected with Gibraltar.
Health
Total expenditure on medical and public health services in 1960 was £182,676 compared with
£22,966 in 1935. The number of beds available in hospitals was :

Colonial Hospital (General) including King George VI Memorial Wing .. 147
King George V Hospital .. 60
Infectious Diseases Hospital .. 10
St. Joseph's Hospital .. 60
The number of in-patients at the Colonial Hospital during 1960 was 2,013 and out-patient attendances numbered 8,033.
Communications

There is no civil airfield in the Colony, but restricted use of the R.A.F. aerodrome at North Front by civil aircraft is permitted, subject to termination if military considerations so require. British European Airways operate daily services between Gibraltar and the United Kingdom both direct and via Madrid. Gibraltar Airways and Royal Air Maroc operate a daily service (two flights) to and from Tangier.
The length of road open for traffic is 5½ miles in the city, 4 miles in the south district and about 3½ miles in the area of North Front and Catalan Bay. All roads are suitable for motor traffic.

The total arrivals of merchant vessels during 1960 were 3,471 with a net tonnage of 9,288,193. The majority called for bunkers, the discharge of cargo and passengers, provisions and repairs. The following passenger services were in operation:

Approximately one cargo vessel a week arrived from the United Kingdom. A daily passenger and car ferry service to and from Tangier is operated by the Bland Line. There is also a daily passenger service between Gibraltar and Algeciras.
Broadcasting

A Government Broadcasting Station (Radio Gibraltar) under the control of the Public Relations Officer broadcasts in English and Spanish for approximately 12 hours daily, an average of 30 hours weekly being devoted to commercial broadcasting. In addition to live and locally recorded programmes, use is made of B.B.C. transcriptions and relays. The power of the station is ½kW and it operates on a frequency of 1,484 kc/s, 202.2 metres. Its coverage is about 50 miles. There is also a local wired broadcasting service operated by the Military Authorities for military personnel, which relays programmes from the B.B.C. During 1960, 4,751 radio receiving licences were issued.
Principal Occupations
Almost half of the male wage-earners in Gibraltar, of whom a large proportion are Spaniards entering for work daily, are employed in one or other of the Service Departments. the Colonial

Government, or the City Council. Commercial enterprises provide employment for a substantial number of clerical workers in shipping offices and trading agencies. Hotels, catering services, retail distributing trades and stevedoring are the other main sources of private employment.

Other local industries are the processing of tobacco, the roasting and blending of coffee, the canning of fish and fruit and the manufacture of ice, mineral water, sweets and various items of clothing.
At the end of December 1960 the total insured labour force was 16,514. There are 20 registered trade unions in the Colony. The reported total paid-up membership of the 16 employees' unions was 4,254.

Tourist Trade
The value of the tourist trade is estimated at about £2,000,000 per annum.

The number of visitors passing through the Colony continues to increase. To meet this increase two luxury hotels are in course of construction. In addition every possible facility is being given. HONG KONG
Area

The area of land is approximately 398$\frac{1}{2}$ square miles (Hong Kong Island 29 square miles, Kowloon 3$\frac{1}{2}$ square miles, Stonecutters Island $\frac{1}{2}$ square mile, New Territories (leased) 365$\frac{1}{2}$ square

miles). It includes all islands within a rectangular area of some 738 square miles, containing the leased and ceded territory. British waters are bounded on the north by the shores of Deep Bay and Mirs Bay, between which lies the land frontier with China.
Population

The population of the Colony at the census held in March 1961 was 3,128,044, of whom the overwhelming majority are of Chinese race, many being British subjects by virtue of birth in the Colony. The number of Europeans and Americans permanently resident, excluding Service personnel and their families, is about 25,500, including some 16,000 British subjects from the United Kingdom and Commonwealth, 1,750 Portuguese, excluding those of British nationality, and approximately 7,750 other aliens.
Principal Towns
The overwhelming majority of the population lives on Hong Kong Island or in Kowloon. The capital is Victoria, on Hong Kong Island.

The principal centres of population in the New Territories are Tsuen Wan, which has recently grown into an important industrial centre with large textile, enamel and rubber factories, iron works, etc.; Cheung Chau, a small but densely populated island, important as a market and fishing centre; Yuen Long, a mainland market town; Tai O, a fishing and market centre on Lantao Island; Tai Po and Shek Wu Hui, both mainland market towns; and Peng Chau, an island fishing port, with some industries.
Geographical Features

The Colony consists of the island of Hong Kong and a portion of the mainland to the North, together with 235 adjacent islands ranging from Lantao with an area of about 58 square miles, to uninhabited rocky islets. A peninsula, on which Kowloon stands, juts southward from the mainland towards Victoria on Hong Kong island. Between these two lies the harbour, one of the finest natural ports in the world. Much of the built-up area surrounding the harbour has been reclaimed or levelled.

The greater part of the Colony consists of steep, unproductive hillside, in some parts covered with dense scrub, in others seriously eroded as a result of indiscriminate felling of trees during the Japanese war-time occupation. Cultivation is confined mainly to the narrow valleys. The coastline is sharply indented.

A steep range of hills divides Kowloon from the New Territories to the North, in the centre of which is the highest mountain—Tai Mo Shan, 3,140 feet; Lantao Peak is 3,061 feet and Victoria Peak on Hong Kong Island 1,805 feet high.
Climate

The climate is sub-tropical and governed by monsoons, the winter being cool and dry, the summer hot and humid. The mean monthly temperature varies from 59° F. in February to 82° F. in July. The actual temperature rarely rises above 95° F. or falls below 40° F. The average annual rainfall is 84.76 inches, three-quarters of which falls between May and September. The mean relative humidity exceeds 80% during the summer but in early winter sometimes falls as low as 20%.
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
History

Hong Kong was founded as a British Colony in 1841, the cession of the Island to Great Britain being confirmed by the Treaty of Nanking in 1842. The area on which the main urban part of Kowloon now stands, together with Stone-cutters Island in the harbour, Ap Lei Chau and Green Island, was ceded by the Convention of Peking in 1860; and in 1898 the New Territories, which consist of the rural area north of Kowloon and the islands around Hong Kong, were leased to Great Britain for 99 years.
The Colony was occupied by the Japanese from 1941 to 1945, and in the following four years made a remarkably rapid recovery.
Constitution

The Government is administered under Letters Patent of 14th February 1917 by a Governor assisted by an Executive Council and a Legislative Council. The Executive Council, which is consulted by the Governor on all important administrative matters, consists of six officials and six unofficials, three of whom are Chinese and one a member of the Colony's Portuguese community. The Legislative Council consists of nine officials and eight unofficials, five of whom are Chinese and one Indian. There is an Urban Council with power to make by-laws relating to public health, sanitation and other subjects. It consists of six officials and 16 unofficials, eight of whom are elected.
Taxation

(1) Earnings and Profits Tax. —This tax, introduced for the first time in 1947 by the Inland Revenue Ordinance (Cap. 112), is a substitute for the more orthodox type of income tax. Instead of one comprehensive tax there are four separate taxes, as follows :
(a) Profits Tax (subdivided into Corporation Profits Tax and Business Profits Tax).
(b) Salaries Tax.
(c) Interest Tax.
(d) Property Tax.

The standard rate for 1961–62 is $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ and this rate is applied in full in the case of Profits Tax and Interest Tax (subject to an exemption limit under Business Profits Tax where tax is normally only charged if the profits exceed $7,000). Salaries Tax is levied on net income from employment after deducting a personal allowance of $7,000, and allowances for a wife ($7,000) and for up to nine children. The rates of tax charged vary from one-fifth of the standard rate on the first $5,000 of chargeable income to double the standard rate on chargeable income exceeding $45,000. There is a provision, however, whereby the maximum charge for Salaries Tax shall not exceed tax at the standard rate on the total assessable income without the deduction of any allowances. Property Tax is charged on the net rateable value of all land and buildings situated in the Colony other than in the New Territories. Effective 1961/62 the charge is at one-half of the standard rate (i.e. $6\frac{1}{4}\%$ ) if the rent receivable is in fact controlled by reference to 1941 rental but at the full standard rate (i.e. $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ ) on all other properties. However, property wholly occupied by the owner exclusively for residential purposes is exempt from Property Tax.
Under all four taxes the charge is limited to profits or income arising in or derived from the Colony. As an alternative to these separate

taxes, a resident of the Colony may elect to be personally assessed on his total Hong Kong income. A single assessment is then made by allowing similar allowances and charging similar rates of tax, as is the case under Salaries Tax. A set-off is then allowed for any amounts already paid under the four separate taxes.

(2) Estate Duty. —Estate Duty is levied on conventional lines at rates varying between 2% in the case of estates valued between $50,000 and $100,000, and 40% in the case of estates valued at over $15 million.

(3) Other Sources of Revenue. —Other sources of revenue are Rates, Stamp Duties, Entertainments Tax, Betting Tax and Business Registration Fees. Excise Duties are also payable on tobacco, hydrocarbon oils, liquor and table waters.
In 1960–61, $237,150,260 was raised from direct and indirect taxes, $166,993,904 from Duties and $100,716,312 from Rates. For the same year the main heads of expenditure were :

Currency
The value of the Hong Kong dollar is maintained at approximately HK $1 = 1s. 3d. sterling.

The currency in circulation consists of $500, $100, $10 and $5 notes issued by three banks of issue: Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corporation, The Chartered Bank and Mercantile Bank, Ltd. respectively. Subsidiary currency consisting of $1 and 1 cent notes and nickel alloy coins in denominations of $1, 50 cents, 10 cents and 5 cents are issued by the Hong Kong Government. The $1 note is gradually being withdrawn from circulation.
Development Plans

Over the next four to five years it is proposed to expend about $2,500 million on development projects, $200 million of which will be provided from the Colony's Development Loan Fund (mainly on housing), and the balance from general revenues.

Education

In 1961 education was provided in 1,864 schools for over 603,000 pupils. This figure represents approximately one in five of the estimated total population. School enrolment may be divided as follows :

Primary .. ..442,500
Secondary .. ..87,300
Post-secondary ..14,500
Other types of Education ..59,500

Private schools provided places for some 334,800 children ; grant-aided and subsidized schools for 178,900; while 103 Government institutions catered for 90,000. In 1960 there were about 1,900 students in the United Kingdom for higher studies, including those doing technical and vocational courses, while a number pursued their studies elsewhere. Government expenditure on education in the Colony was approximately $115 million as compared with $21 million in 1938 and $1 million in 1928.

The University of Hong Kong has 1,264 students in the following faculties—arts, science, medicine, engineering and architecture. The annual Government grant to the University totalled $8.3 million for 1960–61.
Libraries

Local libraries include an English and Chinese library at the University, a British Council library and a small educational library at the Education Department. Various clubs have well-stocked libraries for the use of their members.
Medical and Health

The Health division of the Medical and Health Department undertakes the control of anti-epidemic measures, the care of expectant and parturient mothers, infant welfare work and the inspection and treatment of school children. It is also responsible for port and airport health work, anti-malarial measures, social hygiene, tuberculosis control and a B.C.G. campaign, food and drug control, public health propaganda and pathological, chemical and biochemical laboratory work.

The Government maintains and operates fourteen general and special hospitals and provides financial assistance to ten other hospitals run by voluntary organizations. In addition, there are in the Colony nine private hospitals which do not receive financial help from the Government. The numbers of beds in the three groups (as on 31st March 1961) are 2,432, 3,729 and 1,150 respectively.

In-patients treated in Government and Government-aided hospitals during 1960 totalled over 132,000 and out-patients in the three main Government hospitals were just over 865,800. There are also a large number of dispensaries and clinics in both urban and rural areas.
The general state of health is good; there was no recurrence in epidemic form of influenza during 1960, but a total of 5,727 notifications was recorded.

The most serious scourge is tuberculosis; 12,425 cases were notified in 1960 with 16.8% fatalities. Pneumonia (all forms) occasioned over 3,280 deaths; infant mortality was 41.5 per 1,000 live births.
Government expenditure on these services during the year 1960–61 amounted to approximately $78.5 million, compared with $64.9 million in the previous year.
Capital expenditure on medical projects under the Public Works Non-Recurrent head accounted for another $12.4 million.
Communications

Air.—Hong Kong (Kai Tak) Airport with its new 8,350 feet of runway continues to expand its role in air travel. Since the opening of the runway in September 1958 together with its modern airport lighting system, regularity of aircraft movements has improved considerably. An instrument landing system, together with precision approach radar and long and medium range surveillance radar, has been installed and is operating most successfully and being used continually by airlines.

The increase of air traffic is maintained, both aircraft and passenger handling being higher than the previous year. The principal aircraft types are now Boeing 707 and 720, Comet, Convair 880, Britannia, Viscount and Electra. During 1960, 12,221 international flights by commercial aircraft carried 451,487 passengers, 4,930.7 tons of freight and 1,387.6 tons of mail, in addition to transit traffic.

Rail.—There is a railway 36 kilometres long connecting Kowloon with the Chinese frontier, but there has been no through passenger traffic since October, 1949. Through goods traffic is conveyed in Chinese section wagons. Total goods carried during 1960 amounted to 355,860 metric tons and 5,891,040 passengers were carried.
Roads.—There are 515.5 miles of roadway, 97.44% of which is of metalled construction.

Shipping.—37,485 vessels entered and cleared during 1960–61, their net registered tonnage being 37,444,474; 1,162,147 passengers landed or embarked by sea during the same period. Frequent ferry services cross the harbour and link the principal islands to the westward.
Sound and Television Broadcasting

Radio Hong Kong is the Government-owned broadcasting service. It transmits programmes on medium wave (2 kW) and VHF/FM (5 kW) from two separate stations, in English and four Chinese dialects. In addition Radio Hong Kong operates a short-wave station of 2½ kW. There were 120,151 licence holders at the end of 1960. Rediffusion (Hong Kong), Limited, operate a wired broadcasting service on a commercial basis with programmes in English and Chinese. At the same time, this organization is required to relay a certain proportion of programmes from Radio Hong Kong. At the end of 1960 there were some 44,681 subscribers to the service. Rediffusion Limited also operate a wired television service. A commercial broadcasting organization, the
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Hong Kong Commercial Broadcasting Co., Ltd., transmits programmes on medium wave (1 kW) in English and two Chinese dialects.
Principal Occupations

Before the war, most of the urban population was engaged in commerce, but since 1948 there has been a significant growth of local industries. In June 1961 there were 236,734 workers in 6,072 registered and recorded factories and workshops; 100,714 of them were women. Another 150,000 people are estimated to be employed in smaller cottage-type industries and as outworkers. Reliable statistics of unemployment and under-employment are not obtainable; employment is liable to fluctuation during any year. Few rural families live entirely off the land, farmers' budgets being supplemented by remittances from family members overseas or working in urban jobs in Hong Kong and Kowloon. There is a large fishing population, most of which lives permanently afloat.

The main crops are paddy, vegetables, sweet potatoes and various fruits.

The estimated number of cattle is 20,000 head and of pigs 184,000 head; there are some 3,000,000 head of poultry, including breeding stock and birds for egg production. There are more than 400,000 head of other types of bird, including ducks, pigeons, geese and quail.
In 1960 47,229 tons of fish were landed, as against 49,893 tons in the previous year.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

There are twenty-two Vegetable Marketing Co-operative Societies with a membership of more than 7,500. Twenty-one of these Societies have formed a Federation of Vegetable Marketing Co-operative Societies. In addition, two Irrigation Societies, four Salaried Workers' Thrift and Loan Societies, seven Consumers' and Credit Societies, one Fish Pond Society, 162 Housing Societies, seven Agricultural Credit Societies, four Better Living Societies and 42 Pig Raising Societies have been formed. The last named have also federated and formed a Federation of Pig Raising Societies. Fishermen are becoming more aware of the benefits of co-operation and there are 43 Fishermen's Thrift and Loan Societies, one Fishermen's Thrift Society, one Fishermen's Credit and Marketing Society and two Fishermen's Thrift, Loan and Housing Societies. Forty-one Fishermen's Co-operative Societies have formed themselves into four Federations. The total number of members of Co-operative Societies is more than 17,000.

The Vegetable Marketing Organization transports vegetables to the Central Wholesale Market in Kowloon from co-operative societies, collecting centres and depots in the New Territories. The collecting centres which are run by the farmers have, with the exception of two centres, now developed into co-operative societies.

Five Wholesale Fish Markets are operated by the Fish Marketing Organization. A revolving loan fund with a present ceiling of $1,000,000 has existed since 1946 and is of great benefit to fisherfolk; over $7,670,000 has been loaned from it. The Organization also operates schools for fishermen's children and provides education for more than 2,600 students.

KENYA
Area
The Colony and Protectorate of Kenya is bisected by the equator and extends approximately from latitude $4^\circ$ N. to latitude $4^\circ$ S. and from longitude $34^\circ$ E. to $41^\circ$ E.
The total area is 224,960 square miles (including 5,171 square miles of water).

Geographical Features

Physically, Kenya may be divided into four areas. The north-east is an arid, thorn-bush-covered plain (below 2,000 feet) with a small, nomadic population; the south-east is similar but practically uninhabited except along the banks of the Tana River and in the coastal strip and the
KENYA

Taita Hills (7,000 feet) where rainfall is adequate; the north-west is also generally low and arid but includes Lake Rudolf (160 miles long) and many mountains (e.g. Nyiru 9,200 feet). The south-west quarter, in which 85% of the population and practically all the economic production is concentrated, comprises a plateau rising to 10,000 feet and supporting the mountains Kenya (17,058 feet), Elgon (14,178) and the Aberdare Range (13,104). Much of the area between 7,000 and 11,000 feet is forest (5,000 square miles). The plateau is bisected from north to south by a part of the Great Rift Valley, 30-40 miles wide and 2,000-3,000 feet below the plateau on either side. The Rift floor rises from 1,280 feet at Lake Rudolf to 7,000 feet near Naivasha and falls again to 2,000 feet at Lake Natron. West of the Rift the plateau falls to Lake Victoria (3,720 feet) and eastward the Tana (500 miles) and Athi Rivers flow to the Indian Ocean.
Principal Towns and Port
Mombasa is the centre of the coastal strip and the principal port in East Africa.

History

The eastern and north-eastern boundaries of Kenya were originally defined along the Juba River by an agreement with Italy (1891) but upon the cession of Jubaland to Italy in 1925, a commission was appointed to adjust these boundaries. The northern boundary is defined by an agreement with Ethiopia in 1947 which will supersede the original agreement of 1907, when the demarcated frontier is ratified.

The Protectorate is a strip of land extending ten sea miles inland along the coast from Tanganyika to Kipini and the islands of the Lamu Archipelago and consists of the mainland dominions of the Sultan of Zanzibar, in respect of which an annuity of £16,000 per annum is paid to His Highness. The original concession was made in 1887 to a company, subsequently called the Imperial British East Africa Company, but the administration of the territory was transferred in 1895 to Her Majesty's Government and the whole Protectorate was placed under the control of a Commissioner and Commander-in-Chief. It was transferred in 1905 from the authority of the Foreign Office to that of the Colonial Office. By Orders in Council of 1906 the Protectorate was placed under a Governor and Commander-in-Chief and executive and legislative councils were constituted. The frontiers were defined by agreements with Germany (1890 and 1893) and Italy (1891) and by Order in Council in 1926. All foreign consular jurisdiction was, during the year 1908, transferred to the British court.
By the Kenya Annexation Order in Council, 1920, the territories outside the mainland

dominions of the Sultan of Zanzibar were established as a Colony; the Sultan's mainland dominions are now styled the Protectorate of Kenya, whilst the remaining territory is known as the Colony of Kenya.
Constitution

Certain changes have been made in the Kenya constitution as a result of the Lancaster House Conference held in London during January/February 1960. The changes agreed at this Conference were published in an amending Order in Council, the provisions of which came into force on 7th December 1960 and the 23rd December 1960, respectively. Further changes were made in the amending Orders in Council which came into operation on 31st October and 2nd December 1961.

In accordance with the Kenya (Constitution) Orders in Council, 1958-1961 and the Royal Instructions, the Colony and Protectorate is administered by a Governor and Commander-in-Chief who is advised by a Council of Ministers. There is provision for such number of Ministers as may be prescribed by Her Majesty, by instructions given to the Governor through the Secretary of State. At present, and until Her Majesty otherwise directs by such instructions, the Council of Ministers consists of not more than 12 Ministers, of whom three are civil servants. There are nine unofficial Ministers; five of these are Africans, three are Europeans and one is an Asian. The Deputy Governor is also a Member of the Council of Ministers. There are also Parliamentary Secretaries appointed by the Governor whose duties and functions are to assist Ministers in the exercise of their duties. The Governor's Personal Adviser on Arab Affairs has the right to attend and participate in meetings of the Council of Ministers.

The Legislative Council consists of a Speaker, appointed by the Governor; " Ex Officio Members ", who are Ministers or temporary Ministers, but not otherwise members of the Legislative Council; 53 Constituency Elected Members, of whom 33 are African, 10 European, 8 Asian and 2 Arab; 12 National Members elected by the Council sitting as an Electoral College, of whom 4 are African, 3 are Asian, 4 are European and 1 is an Arab; and a varying number of Nominated Members, at present 11, nominated by the Governor.

A Council of State, designed to protect any community against harmful discriminatory legislation, has also been established under the Order and may consist of a chairman and not more than 16 and not less than 10 members.

Discussions in London during June 1961 at which representatives of Governments participating in the East Africa High Commission took part, have resulted in certain changes. A new body known as the East African Common Services Organisation has been set up and Kenya representation in the new Central Legislative Assembly will consist of 4 Ministers and 9 Members elected by the Legislative Council. A Conference, under the Chairmanship of the Secretary of State, to prepare the constitutional framework for full internal self-government in preparation for independence began in London on 14th February 1962 and a Conference on the future of the 1895 Agreement relating to the Kenya Protectorate also began in London on 8th March. Both Conferences were still meeting at the time of going to press.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Six Municipalities are established under the Municipalities Ordinance, 1928: the City of Nairobi, the Municipal Councils of Eldoret, Mombasa, Nakuru and Kisumu and the Municipal Board of Kitale. All these municipalities now have elected as well as nominated members, the elected members being elected on the basis of Communal Rolls. Seven County Councils are established under the County Councils Ordinance, 1952: Nairobi, Nakuru, Naivasha, Aberdare, Nyanza, Kitale and Uasin Gishu. There are elected and nominated members, the former being elected on Communal Rolls. Thirty-two African District Councils are established under the African District Councils Ordinance, 1950 and they exercise powers and functions similar to those of other local authorities. There are both elected and nominated members and the electors are the ratepayers, i.e. generally excluding women.

Second-tier local authorities have been established in the areas of County Councils, where they are known as Urban or Rural District Councils and in the areas of African District Councils, where they are known as Locational Councils. In a few Townships, Township Accounts are kept, and the Township Committee operates on Local Government principles but without corporate status.

Following the approval of Sessional Paper No. 2 of 1961 by the Legislative Council, a new Local Government Ordinance is now being drafted. The new Ordinance will, in broad terms: (a) provide one legislative framework for all types of local authority; (b) provide for new constitutions for all local authorities; (c) alter the franchise for local government elections and replace communal electoral rolls by common rolls; (d) provide the framework within which African District Councils can progress to the status of County Councils; (e) establish some of the Township Authorities as Township Boards which will be corporate bodies and provide for the inclusion of others within the areas of existing local authorities.
Land Policy

All land in Kenya now falls into one of three categories: (a) Crown lands; (b) Trust lands; (c) Private lands. Crown lands include all leasehold land in the Colony, unalienated land and the Special Reserves, Temporary Special Reserves, Special Leasehold Areas, Special Settlement Areas and Communal Reserves. All unalienated Crown land, whether within a municipality or a township, may be alienated to persons of any race, but in townships and municipalities, the terms of the leases are still restricted to 99 years.

Unalienated Crown land outside municipalities and townships may be granted for agricultural purposes for an initial term corresponding to that required for development, with provision for the grant of freehold (on payment) on completion of the initial development. In pursuance of the Government's policy to place the management and tenure of all land on the same non-racial basis as declared in Kenya Government Sessional Papers Nos. 10 of 1958-59 and 6 of 1959-60, dealings in land in the Declared Areas require the consent of the appropriate Divisional Land Control Board, of which there are twelve, but the question of race is not a factor to be considered. It is now possible for the holder of a leasehold property held from the Crown for agricultural purposes to apply for and be granted a freehold title on payment of 18 times the current annual rent.

The Special Areas (Trust Land) were created by the Kenya (Native Areas) Order in Council, 1939, since superseded by the Kenya (Land) Order in Council, 1960. The Order in Council vests the Special Areas in the Trust Land Board and preserves for the inhabitants thereof all rights and interests enjoyed under native customary law and the occupation, use, control, inheritance, succession and disposal of land is also governed by customary law except in so far as specific provision is contained in the Trust Ordinance (Cap. 100 of the Revised Laws of Kenya). This is still the case in the greater part of the Special Areas to-day, but the Land Registration (Special Areas) Ordinance, enacted in 1959, makes provision for the recognition, by registration, of rights of ownership under customary law as freehold and of lesser rights not amounting to freehold. When land has been registered in private ownership it ceases to vest in the Trust Land Board but that Board is registered as the freehold proprietor of all parcels of land which are not claimed in individual ownership or which are used for public purposes. Individual title has already been registered over large areas of the Kikuyu Districts and parts of the Nandi, Elgeyo/Marakwet and Baringo Districts. In registered areas, except as regards inheritance and succession, native law and custom and the Trust Land Ordinance are superseded by the provisions of the Land Registration (Special Areas) Ordinance and the ordinary land laws of the Colony. The Land Control (Special Areas) Regulations, 1961, provide for control over land transactions to be exercised by Provincial and Divisional Boards, of which the composition is predominantly African. It is the intention gradually to extend this system of registration and control to other parts of the Special Areas where the concept of individual ownership has emerged.
Private land includes all land held in fee simple under either the Land Titles Ordinance, the Crown Lands Ordinance or the Land Registration (Special Areas) Ordinance, 1959.
Taxation

The forms of direct taxation which apply to all communities are income tax and personal tax. The maximum rate of personal tax in 1959 was Shs. 150/-. Under the Income Tax Acts generous personal allowances are given. The rate of tax for resident individuals commences at Shs. 2/- in the £ on the first £400 of chargeable income and increases gradually to a maximum rate of Shs. 15/- in the £ where the chargeable income exceeds £9,000. Persons other than individuals, which includes companies, are liable at Shs. 5/50 in the £. A graduated hospital tax calculated on total income is also payable by all Europeans and Asians.

Currency

The currency in circulation is an East African currency issued and controlled by the East African Currency Board whose headquarters are in Kenya, on behalf of the four East African Territories—Kenya, Uganda, Tanganyika and Zanzibar—and also Aden. The standard coin is the East African shilling (cupro-nickel) with subsidiary coinage of the following denominations: 50 cents (cupro-nickel) and 10 cents, 5 cents and 1 cent (bronze). The note issue consists of notes of the following denominations: Shs. 10,000/-, Shs. 1,000/-, Shs. 100/-, Shs. 20/-, Shs. 10/-, Shs. 5/- (Shs. 200/-, and Shs. 1/- notes previously issued are gradually being withdrawn from circulation and are no longer issued). The currency is interchangeable with sterling at par.
Development Plans

Under the ten-year Development Programme, 1946–55, £38.8 million was allocated, of which £27.4 million was spent over the eight years 1946–53. Of this expenditure 45% was financed from loans, 24% from Colony revenue and 18% from various appropriated funds.

A new Development Programme covered the three and a half years, 1954–57, during which £21.7 million was spent, mainly from loan moneys. Other development expenditure outside the three-and-a-half-year programme amounted to £13.8 million during the same planning period, making a total of £35.5 million in all.

Total expenditure under the 1957–60 Development Programme, which embraced all important development projects, amounted to £26.8 million. Expenditure currently envisaged under the 1960–63 Development Programme amounts to £36.2 million.

Education (1960)

Europeans: 49 primary schools (19 Government, 1 local government, 14 aided, 15 unaided) with altogether 9,455 pupils, 14 secondary schools (6 Government, 2 aided, 6 unaided) with altogether 3,507 pupils.

Asian including Goan and mixed races: 122 primary schools (29 Government, 88 aided and 5 unaided) with altogether 42,023 pupils, 35 secondary schools (12 Government, 11 aided and 12 unaided) with altogether 10,156 pupils, 3 Government teacher-training centres with altogether 355 students.

African: 4,096 primary schools (8 Government, 3,617 aided and 471 unaided) with altogether 619,168 pupils, 928 intermediate schools (10 Government, 894 aided, 24 unaided) with altogether 107,724 pupils, 41 secondary schools (1 Government, 32 aided, 8 unaided) with altogether 5,409 pupils, 42 teacher-training centres (40 aided and 2 unaided) with altogether 4,089 students.

Higher Education
During the year 1960, there were 399 students from Kenya (including 30 Asians and 1 European) studying at Makerere College in Uganda.
The Royal Technical College of East Africa was opened in Nairobi in April, 1956. There are Faculties of Architecture, Arts, Science, Engineering and Commerce and a Department of
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Domestic Science. In 1960 there were 201 Kenya students studying at the College consisting of 13 Europeans, 106 Asians and 82 Africans.
The Royal Technical College will gain University College status for the 1961-62 academic year and will become one of the constituent colleges of the projected University of East Africa.

Many Kenya students are given assistance to take courses of higher education in institutions overseas as well as in Makerere College and the Royal Technical College, Nairobi. Government provides bursaries for all races and additional scholarships are made available from funds allocated under Colonial Development and Welfare schemes. There are also a few scholarships limited to candidates from specific schools. African District Councils award bursaries, mainly for local education, but also in a few cases for study overseas. In addition there are scholarships available for teachers to enable them to take degree and other courses in the United Kingdom and to qualify for responsible teaching posts. Among other sources of funds to assist students going overseas, are scholarships offered by the Governments of India, Pakistan, Ethiopia and Ghana, open to Africans and Arabs, and awards made on the recommendation of a Committee for Study and Training in the United States of America, which are open to all races.

In addition to these awards a new scheme has been started to assist in the higher education of Government servants which provides "Kenya Development Scholarships", open to Kenya Government servants of all races recommended by the Heads of their Departments, to pursue courses which will either improve their capacity as Government servants or fit them for promotion.

An Educational Trust has been formed by a private donor called the Maniben and Mohamedali Rattansi Educational Trust. In the terms of the Trust the income is divided into five equal parts for awards annually to Europeans, Africans, Muslims, non-Muslims and Ismailis. Awards are made from this Trust to students of all races for studies in the United Kingdom, India and elsewhere.

The total number of Kenya students taking courses of higher education in the United Kingdom during 1960 was 1,158. This number included 126 Africans (71 with bursaries, scholarships or loans), 764 Asians and Goans (123 with bursaries, scholarships or loans), 16 Arabs (12 with bursaries, scholarships or loans) and 252 Europeans (84 with bursaries, scholarships or loans).
Trade and Technical Education

There were, in 1959, five Government Technical and Trade Schools for African boys, admitting students who had completed eight years of schooling, for a three or four-year course of training; in 1960, 1,228 indentured apprentices were receiving instruction in building, engineering, tailoring and leather-work trades. Each year 40 boys are selected by competitive examination for admission to Secondary Technical courses leading to the General Certificate of Education of the Associated Examining Board at Ordinary level. Sixteen pupils were admitted for a clerical course.

Vocational training was also given in one aided school and at a number of unaided rural training centres. A number of Government Departments and the East African Railways and Harbours and the Posts and Telecommunications Administra-
tions, conducted training courses for students from the intermediate and secondary schools entering their service.
The Mombasa Institute of Muslim Education, which ranks as a Technical Institute, had 265 students enrolled in 1960.

There were two Asian Technical High Schools providing courses in technical and commercial subjects to School Certificate level. Technical Institute classes in Nairobi were organized in temporary accommodation during 1960; 104 students attended day-release classes in Mechanical and Electrical Engineering subjects and 248 students attended part-time evening classes in Technical Commercial subjects.
Government expenditure on education in 1960-61 was:

Libraries and Museums

Government assists financially the McMillan Memorial Library, which caters for all races, in addition to the Desai Memorial Library, also in Nairobi, and the Seif bin Salim Public Library in Mombasa. In Nairobi the British Council and the United States Information Service maintain lending libraries and reading rooms open to the public. There is a large collection of reference books on natural sciences in the Coryndon Memorial Museum in Nairobi.
The East African Literature Bureau runs book box services for African readers throughout the Colony where bookshops are unknown.

The Coryndon Memorial Museum, Nairobi, is a general natural history museum and also has an important section dealing with prehistory. It ranks as one of the leading museums on the African continent. The Acting Curator, R. H. Carcasson, is assisted by a staff of six Europeans and seventeen Africans.
The Snake Park was opened to the public at the beginning of 1960. It is administered by the Museum Trustees of Kenya and directed by J. H. E. Leakey assisted by a staff of three Africans.

Fort Jesus, Mombasa's three centuries old Portuguese monument, was opened to the public as a museum in 1960. It contains archaeological and historical specimens from the former fortress and from other parts of the African Coast.
Health

The principal causes of mortality are general infectious and parasitic diseases, respiratory diseases, alimentary, allergic, metabolic and blood diseases in that order. Towards the end of 1959 a poliomyelitis epidemic started in Central Province and spread throughout Kenya. The number of cases steadily increased from 57 in October 1959 to 189 in March 1960. An Oral Poliomyelitis Vaccine Campaign commenced in February and ended in December 1960. Some 1,750,000 doses of the Sabin type one vaccine were administered; of these, over 610,000 were given to children under six years old. The estimated Colony population of this latter group is 1.3 million and during this epidemic those affected were mostly African children under the age of two years. By April the peak of the epidemic had passed and it waned until, from August onwards, less than 30 cases per month were reported.
KENYA

In 1960 beds in hospitals (including Government and non-Government hospitals) totalled 10,419. In-patients in Government hospitals totalled 156,877 and out-patients 1,166,765. The development of Health Centres continued and at the end of 1960 130 were in operation.
Total expenditure of the Medical Department in the financial year ending 30th June 1960 was £2,211,444 together with Development expenditure of £269,504.
Communications

East African Airways Corporation operates in Kenya to Mombasa, Kisumu, Eldoret, Kitale and Malindi as well as connecting services to the neighbouring territories of Uganda, Tanganyika and Zanzibar. A number of charter aircraft-operating companies are based at Wilson Airport (formerly called Nairobi West).

All scheduled international and internal services are operated from the new Nairobi airport at Embakasi whilst Wilson Airport (formerly Nairobi West) is retained for smaller charter operations and club flying. Eastleigh Airport is now used by the Royal Air Force mainly as a maintenance base and both their transit and locally based aircraft normally operate from Embakasi.

Air Traffic Control and Telecommunications Services are provided by the Directorate of Civil Aviation, East Africa, and meteorological services by the East African Meteorological Department. Air services operating into East Africa are licensed by the East Africa High Commission.
International services connect Kenya direct with the United Kingdom, Europe, India, South Africa, the Congo, Aden and Madagascar.

In mid-1961 there were approximately 26,000 miles of grant-earning roads; only about 750 are bitumen surfaced. The main roads 3,739 miles, where not bitumenized, are gravel-surfaced and, for the most part, all-weather except during excessive rains. Secondary roads (6,200 miles) are mostly gravel-surfaced, but are more subject to closure to heavy traffic in wet weather. A £4-million Contractor-Finance Scheme for the bitumenization of trunk roads which started in 1959 will be completed in 1963 and a £1-million International Bank Loan Scheme for the improvement of roads in African Areas of high agricultural potential, which started in 1960, will also be completed in 1963.
(See also under "East African Common Services Organization" for Railways and Harbours, Road, Air and Postal and Telegraph Services.)
Broadcasting
Kenya was the first British Colonial Territory to have a regular public wireless broadcasting service. Until 1st October 1959 there were three broadcasting organizations in Nairobi:

(i) Messrs. Cable and Wireless Limited provided services in the English and Asian languages under a Charter granted to them in 1931. This Charter expired in September 1956 but by an agreement between the Company and the Government Cable and Wireless continued to provide these services.
(ii) The Kenya Government through its Department of Information operated a service for African listeners in Swahili and many
vernacular languages. It was known as the African Broadcasting Service and broadcasts from four stations at Nairobi, Mombasa, Kisumu and Nyeri.
(iii) The Forces Broadcasting Station has been operating since 1944 a service for British troops stationed within the Colony.

In 1954 the Kenya Government appointed a Commission, three of whose four members were B.B.C. officials, to make recommendations on the development of broadcasting in Kenya. The Government has drawn up its plans for the future of broadcasting in Kenya on the basis of the Commission's Report and these plans have now been largely implemented with the establishment in 1958 of a Department of Broadcasting, known as the Kenya Broadcasting Service, which took over all the African Services from 1st July 1959 and the English and Asian Services from Cable and Wireless, Ltd., from 1st October 1959.
The Kenya Broadcasting Service provides the following separate services:—
(i) English National Service, broadcasting in English from Nairobi for 91 hours per week, on both short and medium wave VHF/FM;
(ii) Asian National Service, broadcasting in six Asian languages from Nairobi for 68 hours per week, on short and medium wave;
(iii) African National Service, broadcasting in Swahili and English, from Nairobi for 67½ hours per week, on short and medium wave;
(iv) Nairobi Regional Service, broadcasting in Somali, Kikamba, Kikuyu, Kimeru, Masai and Swahili from Nairobi, for 32 hours per week;
(v) West Regional Station, broadcasting in Luo, Kisii, Kalenjin, Luhya, Swahili and English, from Kisumu, for 62 hours per week on short and medium wave;
(vi) Coast Regional Station, broadcasting in Swahili and Arabic from Mombasa, for 58 hours per week, on medium wave only.
The Kenya Broadcasting Service also has a Commercial Service in which advertisements and sponsored programmes are broadcast in the English, Hindustani and Swahili languages.

In the English Service a period of 11½ hrs. per week is at present used for this Service, 8½ hrs. per week for the Asian Service and 11 hrs. per week for the African Service. On the 22nd September 1961, a Bill was prepared making provision for the establishment of the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation to take the place of the Kenya Broadcasting Service. This Bill is to cover future broadcasting and television in Kenya. All the undertaking assets and liabilities of the Kenya Broadcasting Service are to be vested in the Corporation on a date to be fixed by the Governor.
Once this Bill has been enacted, the Corporation will be controlled and governed by a Board which will be independent of the Government.
Principal Occupations
Most of the population of Kenya are occupied in peasant cultivation; a lesser number are pastoralists. Minorities are engaged in seafaring and fishing at the coast and on the Victoria and

In December 1959, 42 employees' and 10 employers' unions were registered under Trade Union legislation. The total paid-up membership of employees' unions, as shown on the 1959 returns, is 46,000. The membership claimed is considerably higher than that figure.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops and products are coffee, tea, sisal, pyrethrum, sodium carbonate, meat, butter, ghee and cheese, hides and skins, maize, wheat and cotton.

The forests of the Highlands provide the bulk of the Colony's timber supplies—softwoods such as Podocarpus and East African pencil cedar and various hardwoods, of which East African olive and East African camphor are those in greatest demand. Forest produce from the established plantations is becoming available in increasing quantities and supplied 23% of the total sales of logs from the Forest Estate during 1960 but there are signs that a general decline is likely. It is too early yet to forecast whether attempts to foster an export trade in charcoal will succeed.
The annual catch in the Kenya waters of Lake Victoria has now declined to some 4,000 tons of

wet fish. The marine fisheries in 1960 produced an estimated 4,250 tons of wet fish worth £166,000 to the fishermen. This yield can be expected to increase steadily in step with the local demand for sea fish.

In 1960 the chief minerals in order of value were cement, soda ash, copper, salt, limestone (other than for cement), refined gold, diatomite, carbon dioxide (natural), graphite and kyanite. The total value of minerals won during 1960 was £5,099,513.

There are in operation in the Colony over 130 main types of manufacturing and industrial concerns which produce goods both for internal consumption and export. There has been a steady rise in the export of Kenya's products, which, over the past five years, have increased by more than 20% in value to £35 million in 1960. The main items of export in 1960 were coffee, sisal, tea, pyrethrum, meat and meat preparations, hides and skins and soda ash. Two cement factories, in addition to supplying home requirements, exported more than 42,000 tons during 1960, nearly 70% more than during 1959.
(Including bullion and specie, excluding all inter-territorial transfers of domestic produce to Uganda and Tanganyika.)

In 1960, 34.1% of total direct imports came from the United Kingdom, 14.9% from other Commonwealth countries and 46.6% from foreign countries; postal packages and special transactions accounted for 4.4%.
In 1960, 25.1% of domestic exports went to the United Kingdom, 19.7% to other Commonwealth countries, and 54.6% to foreign countries; 0.6% exported as ships' and aircraft stores.

Tourist Trade

Kenya's share of the East African income from tourism in 1960 is estimated at approximately £4,100,000 and so it ranks as the country's second largest "export". Visitors arriving in Kenya during 1960 totalled 35,800 and stayed an average of nearly 19 days.
Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

On 31st December 1960 there were 625 registered co-operative societies, with a total membership of 175,310. Of these, 546 were marketing societies, which include African, Asian and non-racial societies. There are no longer any European societies, as all these Colony-wide organisations have opened their doors to all races. The value of the produce handled by marketing societies during the year was £21,200,000 which was an increase of £1,700,000 over the previous year.

MALTA, G.C.
Area

The total area is 122 square miles made up of the three main islands as follows : Malta 94.876 square miles, Gozo 25.899 square miles and Comino 1.075 square miles. Filfla and Cominotto are mere islets.
Population
The population of Malta is European; on the 31st December 1960 the total population was estimated to be 328,938 divided as follows: Malta 301,247, Gozo and Comino 27,691.
Malta is overpopulated and it is the policy of the Government to encourage and assist emigration. From 1947 to 1959, 71,684 persons emigrated; the figure for 1960 was 3,841.

Geographical Features
Malta consists of a plateau with a hard core of coralline limestone on the west side which descends by gentle steps to a plain composed of a
MALTA

thick stratum of soft globigerine limestone on the south-east. The highest point on the Island, situated near Dingli, is just over 800 feet above sea level. The Grand Harbour and Marsamxett Harbour are excellent ports of call, while safe anchorages are also provided by Marsaxlokk Bay, St. Paul's Bay and Mellieha Bay. There are no rivers, lakes or forests. In low-lying places the soil, which contains a high percentage of lime and is rich in phosphates, is deep, but elsewhere it is shallow. Cultivation is mainly in terraced fields where the soil is held by rubble walls.
Climate

The climate is healthy. There are no excessive periods of hot or cold weather. The lowest temperature in 1960 was $40^{\circ}$ F. recorded on the 4th February, while the highest, $98^{\circ}$ F., occurred on the 30th August. The mean temperature for 1960 was $66.7^{\circ}$ F. and the mean annual rainfall was 12.92 inches. The mean annual rainfall at Valletta for the past ten years is 22.71 inches. Rain is rare in June, July and August. In August the heat at sea level approaches that experienced in subtropical countries. The nights, however, are pleasant and cool, except during the prevalence of the sirocco wind which blows from the south-east.
History

From Roman times until 1530 Malta was subjected successively to Arab and Sicilian domination. In 1530, the Emperor Charles V gave the Island to the Knights of St. John of Jerusalem, who held it until 1798, when Napoleon Bonaparte invested it on his way to Egypt, having prepared the ground by intrigue and revolutionary propaganda. On 12 June 1798 Ferdinand de Hompesch, the last Grand Master in Malta, capitulated without any resistance and the Order was dispersed. The French installed themselves in the islands, but their interference with Maltese laws and customs brought about a sharp reaction, and the Maltese drove the French troops within the fortified towns and, with the help of the British fleet, held them closely blockaded for two years, after which the French, reduced to extremities, surrendered to the British.

In 1800 the Government was placed in the hands of Great Britain. The Treaty of Amiens provided that the islands should be restored to the Knights, but this was prevented by the recommencement of the war and they were finally annexed to the British Crown by the Treaty of Paris in 1814.

Malta was a vital point in allied strategy in the Second World War and the Island was subjected to heavy enemy air attacks between the entry of Italy into the war in 1940 and the liberation of North Africa in 1943. In 1942, Malta was awarded the George Cross in recognition of the part she had played in the War.
Constitution

Following the resignation of Mr. Mintoff's Government on the 24th April 1958, a State of Emergency was declared in Malta on the 30th April 1958, primarily to enable the Governor to continue the administration of Government in the absence of Ministers. A new constitution was
introduced on the 15th April 1959 which is contained in the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1959 and the Royal Instructions issued on the 30th March 1959.

Provision is made under the Constitution for an Executive Council, presided over by the Governor, consisting of the Chief Secretary, the Legal Secretary and the Financial Secretary as ex officio members and of such other nominated members (appointed by the Governor) of whom not less than three shall be persons holding a public office.

In accordance with Her Majesty's Government's undertaking that this constitution would be interim, a constitutional commission was appointed for Malta at the end of July 1960, to submit recommendations for a return to representative government. The commission's report was published in February 1961 (Cmnd. 1261) and a constitution based on its recommendations was promulgated on the 24th October 1961 as the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1961. The electoral provisions only of the Constitution were brought into force on 1st November 1961 and it was subsequently announced that elections would take place between the 17th and 19th February 1962. The remaining sections of the Constitution, including that providing for the appointment of a United Kingdom Commissioner, are to be brought into force by the Governor after the formation of an elected Government.
Land Policy
There is no restriction on the alienation of land to non-Maltese but the Mortmain Law restricts the alienation of land to religious bodies or institutions under certain circumstances.
Taxation
The principal forms of taxation are income tax, import duties, excise duties, stamp duties, entertainment duties, tombola duty, licence duties and succession and donation duties.
Income tax, introduced in 1949, is levied on all personal incomes, with certain exceptions, at the following rates :—
2s. for every £ of the first £200.
3s. for every £ of the next £300.
4s. 6d. for every £ of the next £400.
6s. 6d. for every £ of the remainder.
The tax on companies is at the rate of 5s. on every £ of the chargeable income.
A surtax is also levied on incomes exceeding £2,500 at the following rates:—
2s. for every £ of the first £500.
3s. for every £ of the next £500.
4s. for every £ of the next £500.
5s. for every £ of the remainder.

Interest at 2½% is paid by the Government on £78,281 outstanding balance on 31st March 1961 of the debt known as "Massa Frumentaria" incurred by the Municipality of Valletta under the Government of the Order of St. John of Jerusalem, in connexion with the monopoly of the wheat trade. The funds of the "Massa Fru-
mentaria" were in 1798 appropriated by the French Government then in occupation of the Island.

Under the Malta Treasury Bills Act, 1952, which has since been superseded by the Malta Treasury Bills Emergency Ordinance, 1958, the Government whenever authorized by Resolution of the Legislative Assembly, could raise money not exceeding the amount specified in such Resolution by means of three months' Treasury Bills. On 31st March 1961 the amount so raised was £1,415,000.

The Government is also empowered to issue loans up to £3,000,000 under the Local Loans (Registered Stock and Securities) Ordinance, 1959. In December 1959 £1,300,000 Local Development Registered Stocks were issued at £98 per cent. bearing 6% interest and in October 1960 a further £1,200,000 stocks were issued at £95 per cent. bearing 6% interest. Both amounts were fully subscribed.
Currency

The currency of Malta consists of notes of 20s. and 10s., issued by the Board of Commissioners of Currency, together with British coins, including silver, cupro-nickel and bronze coins and local notes in denominations of 5s., 2s. 6d., 2s. and 1s. Bank of England notes ceased to be legal tender in the Island in 1949. Maltese £1 and 10s. notes are on a par with sterling and £ for £ coverage is maintained at the Bank of England. The amount of local currency in circulation on 31st March 1961 was £22,292,000.
Development Plans
During the financial year ended 31st March 1961, a total of £4,336,659 was spent on development and other capital works.

Since the end of the War, direct aid by way of free grant from Her Majesty's Government to Malta has amounted to about £50 million, mainly in respect of capital expenditure including War Damage and Reconstruction grants.

The Five-Year Development Plan for the Maltese Islands covering the period 1959–64, which was published in October 1959, was reviewed towards the beginning of the third year. The Reviewed Plan was published in July 1961 and provides for an expenditure of over £35½ million of which £29¼ million is being made available by Her Majesty's Government in loans and grants, including £19¼ million from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds. It is also hoped to secure a long term loan of £2.3 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development to finance the cost of a new power station and distillation plant.

The Plan aims at diversifying the Maltese economy which has hitherto been largely sustained by the expenditure of the fighting Services; this expenditure is bound to decline because of changing defence policies and concepts. The measures contemplated in the Plan to achieve this end may be summarized as follows:—

(a) The Harbour, which is Malta's leading natural asset, is to be developed by the conversion of the Dockyard to commercial use and by the construction of modern deep water quays and other port facilities. A £7¼ million loan from Colonial Development and Welfare sources is being made for the Dockyard operation which has been entrusted to a United Kingdom firm of ship
Digitized by
MALTA
repairers. Work on the new quays and other port facilities is well under way and it is expected that it will be completed by the end of 1961.

(b) Funds are provided for the building of a fully serviced industrial estate, work on which is now well advanced, and it is expected that some fourteen factories will have been built by March 1962. Capital loans and grants are also offered to industry, in addition to an income tax "holiday" and Customs duties easements available under the Aids to Industries Ordinance. An extensive publicity campaign undertaken by an Aids to Industries Board has drawn the attention of industrialists in the United Kingdom, on the Continent and elsewhere to the wide range of aid offered. The results have been very successful and some 20 projects for different industries have been approved. Besides these direct aids, the Plan also sets out to provide the basic services necessary for industrialization apart from the port already referred to above, i.e., the expansion of the water and electricity supplies (work on a new power station and water distillation plant is expected to be taken in hand this year) and the modernization and expansion of the telephone services. Technical Education is also to be developed in order to meet industry's increasing needs for skills. A Polytechnic is to be constructed and a grant of £100,000 has been secured from U.N.E.S.C.O. towards furniture and equipment.

(c) The tourist industry is being built up to take advantage of Malta's climatic, scenic and historical assets. The Capital loans and grants to industry referred to above will also be made available for hotels. The extension of essential services to hotel resort sites is being undertaken by the Government and for this purpose just under £1,000,000 is being provided.
(d) The two indigenous industries, agriculture and fisheries, are being assisted towards greater efficiency.

(e) Although the greater share of the funds is allocated to these economic objectives, substantial provision is made for the social services—primary and secondary education, health, housing, sewers and public buildings.
Education

Compulsory education was introduced in 1946. Primary education is carried on principally in free Government schools which are Roman Catholic. There are 114 Primary schools, where Maltese and English are the only languages of instruction, with an enrolment of 53,203 pupils; and six Grammar schools with 3,652 pupils. The Royal University of Malta, founded in 1769, has an average attendance of 388 students and grants degrees and diplomas in divinity, law, medicine, architecture, engineering, dental surgery, pharmacy, arts and science. The number of Maltese studying or attending post-graduate courses in United Kingdom Universities or colleges during 1960 was 48. There are 31 evening classes for languages and commercial subjects with an attendance of 529 and 67 literacy classes for illiterates and semi-illiterates with an attendance of 845. There are three Secondary Technical Schools providing five-year courses leading to G.C.E. and a Sixth Form for 1,288 pupils of whom 419 are girls. One Technical school for craft training—admission 14 to 17 years—provides a three-year course for 596 pupils. An apprenticeships school and a technical college provide five-year courses for 461 apprentices on the day release system. St. Patrick's Residential School caters for

90 boarders, age 9 to 15. The School of Art provides seven evening classes attended by 185 persons of all ages. Evening classes mainly for emigrants have an average attendance of 1,650 persons and 206 persons are undergoing accelerated full-time technical training in various trades, at two Government Industrial Training Centres in Malta and one in Gozo. These centres were started under the vocational training scheme for prospective migrants. There is a Government home for girls with an average total of 54 inmates. The total number of private (primary and secondary) schools is 88 with an attendance of about 18,444 pupils.
In 1960–61 expenditure on education was £1,547,012 (of which £537,396 was capital expenditure). Expenditure on education in 1935 was £149,521.
Libraries, Museums, etc.

The Royal Malta Library including the Juvenile Library has a stock of 56,172 works, the Gozo Public Library has 16,760. There are also 48 district circulating libraries in the various towns and villages (35 in Malta and 13 in Gozo).

The Museum at Valletta was closed during the war. The collections are being transferred to the Auberge de Provence where the National Museum was set up and opened to the public on 12th January 1958. The following museums and monuments are open to visitors:—

The National Museum, Valletta. Ghar Dalam, Cave and Museum, Birzebbuga. Hal Saflieni Prehistoric Hypogeum, Paola. Tarxien Prehistoric Temples. Hagar Qim Prehistoric Temples. Mnajdra Prehistoric Temples. Museum of Roman Antiquities and Roman Villa, Rabat. St. Paul's Catacombs, Rabat. The Palace Armoury, Valletta. The Gozo Museum, The Citadel, Gozo. Ggantija Prehistoric Temples, Xagħra.
Health

There are seven Government hospitals in Malta with a total of 2,590 beds and five in Gozo with 456 beds. During 1960 in-patients numbered 16,120, out-patient attendances amounted to 71,218. There are in addition 42 District Medical Officers, 48 Health Visitors (district nurses) and five subsidized Midwives. In the Government dispensaries the number of attendances by the public in 1960 was 105,403.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Communications

The international airport of Malta is Luqa Airfield, which is also a Royal Air Force Station. There are other Service airfields at Hal Far and Ta' Qali. British European Airways operate the following scheduled services:—(a) Twenty one services a week to London. These call at Palermo or Ajaccio or Naples or Alghero or at Rome and Nice; (b) three services a week to Catania; (c) daily services to Tripoli; and (d) three services a week to Benghazi via Tripoli.

Skyways Limited, under a pooling agreement with British European Airways operate a scheduled service between Malta and London via Tunis twice weekly. British United Airlines operate coach class services once every two weeks between Malta and East and Central Africa, and freight services between Malta and East, Central and South Africa, and between Malta, Frankfurt and London.
During 1960, 2,650 aircraft offloaded 48,261 passengers and picked up 50,136 others. 38,589 passengers passed through Malta in transit.
The size of Malta does not provide scope for internal air services and the only railway line ceased to operate in 1931.

Ten miles of roads have been resurfaced with hot-laid bitumen macadam. Surfacing for main roads remains penetration macadam (semi-grouting) and 415 miles are paved in this method using limestone aggregate and bituminous emulsion; 157 miles of country and estate roads are paved in water-bound macadam.
The number and tonnage of ships which entered the Grand Harbour during 1960 are:—
2,021 Steamships, tonnage 3,273,916.
94 Sailing ships, tonnage 8,544.

There is no regular passenger line between Malta and the United Kingdom. The Italian Tirrenia Line runs a regular passenger service between Malta and the Continent calling at Syracuse, Catania and Naples. (Frequency: June–October every 3 days; September–May every 5 days) and the Laferla Shipping Line which runs a Service between Malta and Syracuse from April to mid-October (Frequency: varying 2 to 6 sailings per week according to traffic offering).
Broadcasting

Messrs. Rediffusion (Malta) Ltd. operate a wired sound broadcasting service which was first introduced in 1935. The number of subscribers on 31st July 1961 was 47,562. As on the same date there was a total of 22,041 licences for wireless receiving sets (including 12,629 television sets). Although there is as yet no television broadcasting station in Malta, Italian T.V. transmissions can be received; preparations are also far advanced for the setting up of a local station by Malta Television Service Limited.

In 1958 a Board was appointed by the Governor to organise political and other controversial broadcasting. This was wound up in September 1961 in anticipation of the setting up of the Malta Broadcasting Authority, a statutory public corporation which alone is vested with the function of providing, either directly or through contractors, both sound and television services in Malta. An Ordinance (No. XX of 1961) creating the Authority was enacted by the Governor on 29th July 1961 and was brought into force on 29th September 1961, as from which date the Authority was formally established.
Principal Occupations

The gainfully-occupied population (88,690) was engaged in 1960 in the following occupations: agriculture (7,520), fishing (600), stone quarrying and clay pits (630), manufacturing and mechanical (14,960), construction (7,890), commerce (11,860), communications (4,050), community, recreation and personal services (8,130), Government (17,000), dockyard and Service Departments, including local servicemen (15,930).

On 30th June 1960 there were 61 registered trade unions with a total membership of 29,776, fifteen combinations of employers with a membership of 2,221 and six combinations of employers and employees with a total of 2,409 members.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are potatoes, onions, grapes, wheat, barley and forages. Fresh vegetables and potatoes are the main cash crops.
The livestock population in 1960 was: Cattle 9,302; horses 1,875; donkeys 2,573; mules 1,672; sheep 13,016; goats 34,688; pigs 14,146; poultry 344,483; rabbits 52,545.
During 1960, 1,237 tons of fish were landed by local fishermen (mainly during the months between May and November) with a first sale value of £227,000.
Malta has to import nearly all the raw materials required for industry.
The two leading industries are the ship-repair yard—formerly a Royal Naval establishment privately operated since April 1959—and the building industry.

Besides a number of light industries produce, flour, macaroni, edible oil, tomato paste, canned fruits and vegetables, beer, soft drinks, wine; buttons, footwear, gloves, ladies' handbags, knitwear, homespun lace, nylon socks and stockings, shirts and pyjamas, textiles, leather goods; cigarettes, pipes and other smoking requisites; candles, matches, paints, bituminous emulsion, mirrors, crown corks, nails and screws, neon-signs, refrigerators, boats, brooms; flowers and seeds.

Following the grant of assistance under the Aids to Industries Scheme, these new industries have been set up, namely: bacon; detergents; carnation and chrysanthemum cuttings; hand-made imitation jewellery; expanded plastic materials; synthetic rubber seals; synthetic yarn processing; plastisol articles.

The Government currently grants exclusive rights to produce goods under the provisions of the Encouragement of New Industries Act only in those cases where the industry is of exceptional importance to the development of the economy of the Island.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations
The marketing of agricultural produce is controlled by the Agricultural Produce (Marketing) Regulations 1952.

Fresh fruit and vegetables are sold by wholesale auctions under Government supervision at 6 Marketing Centres run by 23 "Pitkali" or middlemen and by Co-operative marketing societies. The former charge a commission of $6\frac{1}{2}\%$ whilst the latter charge only $4\frac{7}{12}\%$ . Government makes a contribution to the Co-operative marketing societies at the rate of $3\frac{3}{4}\%$ .

In 1960 there were 15 registered agricultural producers co-operative societies, 10 of which specialised in marketing and represented by a central society. There were also 2 milk producers, 2 agricultural equipment, 1 wine producer and 2 consumers co-operative societies. 2 other non-agricultural societies were in course of liquidation.

MAURITIUS
Area

The area of Mauritius is approximately 720 square miles, that of Rodrigues 40 square miles and the area of the Lesser Dependencies, viz., the Chagos Archipelago (the largest islet is Diego Garcia), Agalega and the Cargados Carajos or St. Brandon Isles, approximately 47 $\frac{1}{2}$ square miles.
Population

The estimated population at 31st December 1960 was: Mauritius 648,838, Rodrigues 17,662, Lesser Dependencies 1,950; divided approximately into: (1) general population, comprising Europeans and descendants of Europeans and people of African and mixed origin, 29%; (2) Indo-Mauritians, made up of immigrants from the Indian subcontinent and their descendants, 68%; and Chinese, consisting of immigrants from China and their descendants, 3%.
Principal Towns
At the 31st December 1960 the estimated population of the principal towns was:—

Port Louis (capital) .. 89,900
Curepipe (new limits) .. 37,000
Beau Bassin-Rose Hill .. 36,400
Quatre Bornes .. 22,400
Geographical Features

The island lies in the Indian Ocean, 550 miles east of Madagascar. It is chiefly of volcanic formation but almost entirely surrounded by coral reefs. There are several small chains of mountains, the principal peaks reaching about 2,700 feet. There are numerous rivers and streams; none of them are navigable beyond a short distance from the sea. Some of the larger waterfalls have been harnessed to supply hydro-electric
MAURITIUS

power. The land rises gradually from the coasts and the centre of the island is composed chiefly of a fertile plateau varying in altitude from 1,000 feet to 2,000 feet. Remnants of the native forests of the island cover some 15,000 acres.
Climate

The south-east trade winds blow during most of the year and the climate is generally humid. The mean temperature varies from $74^{\circ}\text{F.}$ at sea level to $67^{\circ}\text{F.}$ at 2,000 feet. The highest and lowest values ever recorded at Pamplemousses at 180 feet are $95^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and $50^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and at Vacoas, at 1,394 feet on the western slopes, are $98^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and $46^{\circ}\text{F.}$

Rainfall differs considerably according to the height above sea level. On the highest part of the central plateau an average of 200 inches falls each year but amounts decrease rapidly to 60 and 35 inches on the windward and leeward coasts. There is no marked dry season but in the three months from September to November rainfall is about one quarter of the fall in the three wettest months, January to March.

Tropical cyclones may occur between September and May but by far the greatest frequency and intensity is in January and February; March and April also being important months owing to the greater susceptibility to damage of the grown sugar cane. In about 10% of all years, mean wind speed over an hour reaches 50 m.p.h. and gusts 100 m.p.h.; gusts of 80 m.p.h. may be expected about 25 times every century.
