ZANZIBAR
Area
Zanzibar Island is 640 square miles in area and Pemba 380 square miles.
Population
The mid-1960 estimate of population was 307,000. At the 1958 census the population
was 299,111 (Zanzibar Island 165,253 and Pemba 133,858); racial composition:

Indigenous inhabitants, Arabs, mainland Africans and Comorians 279,935
Asians other than Arabs .. .. 18,334
Europeans .. .. .. .. 507
Somalis and others .. .. .. 335

Principal Towns Population (1958 census)
Zanzibar Township .. .. 57,923
Wete (Pemba) .. .. .. 7,507
Chake Chake (Pemba) .. .. 7,167
Geographical Features

The island of Zanzibar is the largest coralline island on the East African coast and is separated from the mainland by a channel 22½ miles across at its narrowest part. To the north-east at a distance of some 25 miles lies the island of Pemba. Both islands are low-lying, the highest land being little above 390 feet (Masingini Ridge).
Climate

The mean maximum temperature is 84.4° and the mean minimum 76.6° (Zanzibar Town). The warmest season is from January to March (average 82° to 86°) and the coolest, June to September (average 75° to 78°). The average annual rainfall in Pemba varies from 65 inches in the north to 92 inches in the south; in Zanzibar from 49 inches to over 72 inches, the maximum being recorded in the centre of the Island. There is no real dry season but the rainy seasons are well marked, being March to May in the south-west monsoon and in November and December at the start of the north-east monsoon.
History

In 1698 the Arabs of Oman captured Mombasa, where the Portuguese had built a fort in 1592. Thereafter the whole of the East African coast as far south as Cape Delgado, including Zanzibar and Pemba, passed from the Portuguese into the hands of the Arabs. Seyyid Said bin Sultan, the founder of modern Zanzibar and of its clove industry, became ruler of Oman in 1805 and later transferred his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar. During his reign Zanzibar became both politically and commercially the principal town in East Africa. In 1861 by an award of Lord Canning, then Governor-General of India, the ruler of Oman's possessions in Africa became independent and from that date Oman and Zanzibar have remained politically separate.
By an agreement in 1886 Great Britain and Germany recognized the Sultanate of Zanzibar over the islands of the coast (including Zanzibar and Pemba) and a ten-mile wide strip on the mainland.

In 1887 the Sultan handed over the administration of his possessions to the north of Vanga on the African continent to the British East Africa Association (later, the Imperial British East Africa Company). The Government of these territories eventually passed to the British Government and they are now known as the Kenya Protectorate. Zanzibar still receives the sum of £16,000 a year from Kenya for the rights which the latter exercises in the coastal strip.
In June 1890 Zanzibar and Pemba were placed under British protection by the Sultan, Seyyid
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Ali bin Said. The Protectorate was proclaimed in that year and the following year a regular form of Government was set up.

In 1913 the control of the Sultanate passed from the Foreign Office to the Colonial Office, legal effect being given to the change in 1914 by a new Zanzibar Order in Council under which the offices of His Majesty's Agent and Consul-General and His Highness's First Minister were merged in that of the newly created post of British Resident who was appointed subject to the control of the Governor of the British East Africa Protectorate as High Commissioner. A Protectorate Council was constituted as an advisory body with His Highness as President and the British Resident as Vice-President.

In 1925 the office of High Commissioner was abolished and the British Resident was made directly responsible to the Colonial Office. Executive and Legislative Councils were constituted in 1926 in place of the old advisory Protectorate Council.
The present Sultan acceded in 1960.
Constitution

The Government is administered by the British Resident who exercises his functions under the Zanzibar Order in Council, 1924. The Privy Council is presided over by the Sultan; the Heir Apparent, the British Resident, the Chief Minister, the Civil Secretary and the Attorney-General are members.

As a result of the elections for Legislative Council held in June 1961, a Government was formed by the Zanzibar Nationalist Party and the Zanzibar and Pemba People's Party. Legislation provides for an Executive Council presided over by the British Resident and consisting of three ex officio members and five elected members, one of whom is the Chief Minister. The Legislative Council is presided over by a Speaker appointed from outside the Legislature and consists of three ex officio members, twenty-three elected members and five appointed members.
Land Policy

Under the Land Alienation Decree of 1939, subject to certain provisos, permanent alienation of land, leases exceeding two years and mortgages, by Arabs or Africans have no effect without the consent of a Land Alienation Board. In certain of the more undeveloped areas of the island, mainly inhabited by the indigenous Africans, the land is communally held and, according to native custom, cannot be alienated, although this custom has given way to freehold tenure in some places where plantations of economic trees have been established. Throughout the plantation areas (west and centre of both islands) land is freehold and there is no other statutory qualification as to its disposal. A good deal of land is, however, reserved under the system of quasi-religious dedication of Muslim law known as wakf .
Taxation
The principal forms of taxation are Customs import duty, clove duty and income tax. There is no poll or hut tax.

The Income Tax Decree provides for taxation on incomes of over £225. With effect from the 1958 income year, tax on income from agricultural sources is charged. The rate of taxation is in a graduated scale of Sh. 2 in the £ on the first £400 of taxable income, rising to Sh. 1 in the £ on each
further £400 up to £2,000; thence on each further £500 up to £3,000; and on each £1,000 up to £9,000; on the excess over £9,000 Sh. 15 in the £ is charged.
Public Finance

Public Debt: (1) a loan of £193,713 from the National and Grindlays Bank Finance and Development Corporation Limited for the Zanzibar Electricity Scheme; (2) £500,000 of 6½% Zanzibar Stock 1970–72 issued locally in Zanzibar in 1960.

The main heads of expenditure in 1960 were Agriculture £134,437; Education £380,009; Health £305,018; Pensions £221,482; Police £159,077; Port and Marine £118,401; Provincial Administration £124,584; Public Works £359,614. The total Development Expenditure was £232,797.

The chief sources of revenue in 1960 were Import Duty £739,803, Cloves and Stems Duty £853,976, Licences and Taxes £253,971, Court Fees, etc. £30,991, Earnings and Sales of Government Departments £248,496, Interest and Redemption £147,713 and Colonial Development and Welfare Grants £29,817.
Currency

The standard coin of the Protectorate is the East African Currency Board shilling (Shs. 20 = £1) with the following subsidiary coinage:— 50 cents (cupro-nickel) and 10 cents, 5 cents, 1 cent (bronze). East African Currency Board notes are legal tender for the amount expressed thereon.
Development Plans

Zanzibar has received grants to a total of £1,369,794 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts in connexion with the Ten-Year Development Programme. From these grants £974,716 was issued up to 31st December 1955. The programme was revised in 1951 and since that time practically all the permanent recurrent expenditure has been taken into the Protectorate Estimates.

A new development plan was made in 1955 to cover the years 1955–59. This provided for total expenditure of £1,300,000 of which £909,000 is to be found from Protectorate sources, the remainder coming from Colonial Development and Welfare funds, of which approximately £291,000 was carried forward from the Ten-Year Development Programme.
In 1959 a development plan was made for the period 1960–64 but was not in fact adopted and is still under consideration.
Education
The following schools are maintained by Government :

Schools Pupils
Boys Girls Boys Girls

Muslim Academy 1 — 142 —
ZANZIBAR

In addition there are 10 Assisted primary schools (1,073 boys and 1,936 girls) and 5 private primary schools (214 boys and 53 girls), 2 Assisted secondary schools (186 boys and 106 girls), 2 Government Teacher Training schools (79 men and 30 women), 6 private kindergarten schools and 906 Kuran schools with an enrolment of 17,000 pupils.

The Assisted schools are mainly for Indians, and include those run by various Christian Missions. A Trade school was started in 1956 to train boys for the building trade. Primary education in Government schools is free for Arabs and Africans. Post-secondary education is not yet available in the Protectorate and students proceed to Makerere College, Uganda, or elsewhere. Scholarships are provided for selected scholars to go principally to the United Kingdom and India. In 1960 more than 70 such scholars were being supported by Government grants.
European children usually attend schools on the mainland or elsewhere. Parents receive financial assistance from the Government.
Expenditure on education in 1960 was £397,679 compared with £27,220 in 1935.
Health

Preventive measures include the normal controls on immigration, environmental sanitation, anti-malarial control, the supervision of buildings, the maintenance of District Rural Units, District Maternity Units, antenatal clinics and a School Dental Service.
The distribution of beds in the various hospitals throughout the Protectorate is as follows:

Hassanali Karimjee Jivanjee Hospital 273
Zenubbai Karimjee Hospital, Dole, Zanzibar 40
Holmwood Mental Hospital, Zanzibar 185
Zenubbai Karimjee Hospital, Wete, Pemba 81
Chake Chake Hospital, Pemba 43
Mkoani Hospital, Pemba 23

Prison Hospital, Zanzibar Town 17
Walezo Leprosarium and Old Folks Home, Zanzibar 100
Makondeni Leprosarium, Pemba 100

Makunduchi Maternity Centre, Zanzibar 6
In addition to the above, the Zanzibar Maternity Association, with the assistance of an annual grant from the Government, maintains Mwembeladu Maternity Home with 20 beds.
Expenditure on Health in 1960 was £305,016; in 1935 it was £35,577.
Communications

Zanzibar airport is situated four and a half miles from the town. It has one all-weather runway. Operations are restricted to aircraft with a maximum all-up weight of 44,000 lb. This figure is ample for the types of aircraft in use at present, but the construction of the runway and the adjacent terrain is such that extensions could easily be made. Air Traffic Control and other services are provided. The airport is open during daylight hours. Pemba Airport is situated approximately six miles from Chake Chake. There is an all-weather runway with a landing restriction of 33,000 lb. Only temporary terminal facilities exist at present.
Zanzibar has 387 miles of roads, of which 276 miles are bitumen surfaced; Pemba has 227 miles, of which 81 are bitumen surfaced. There are no railways.

Ocean-going vessels maintain regular and frequent communication with all parts of the world. Government steamers maintain regular weekly connexions with Pemba and Dar es Salaam. Coasting vessels, carrying both passengers and cargo, also maintain regular services between Zanzibar, Kenya and Tanganyika ports.
Broadcasting

The Government broadcasting service provides a four hours' programme in Kiswahili six days a week. Transmission is on 49.96 metres at $3\frac{1}{2}$ kW and 500 metres at $\frac{1}{4}$ kW. The medium wave transmitter is owned by the Government. The shortwave transmitter is the property of Cable and Wireless Limited who operate both from the station at Marahubi under an agreement with Government. It is estimated that there are some 10,000 receiving sets in the Protectorate.
Principal Occupations

A large proportion of the rural population is engaged in food production; paid labour is mainly dependent on the growing, harvesting and processing of the Protectorate's two major products, cloves and coconuts.
Around the coast, fishing is an important occupation normally followed in conjunction with agriculture.
In urban areas, the principal occupations are transport, building, port and dock labour, domestic service, sanitation and public works, clerical and business.

During 1959 existing trade unions were required by the 1958 Trades Unions Decree to re-register. By the end of the year, nine unions with a combined membership of 2,665 had done so, and a further five unions had applied and were awaiting the decision of the Registrar of Trade Unions.
Main Crops and Products

The main crops are cloves, coconuts, rice, cassava, sweet potatoes, citrus fruits, chillies, tobacco, maize and millet. Cocoa production is being encouraged and is being planted to an increasing extent.
The number of cattle in Pemba is approximately 32,000 and in Zanzibar 15,000. The export of hides and skins is a useful item in the Protectorate's economy.

There are two small forests, one in Zanzibar and one in Pemba which yield a small amount of Mfuu ( Vitex cuneata ) and Mtondoo ( Calophyllum inophyllum ) and other species, while jackwood ( Artocarpus integra ) is a useful timber of the clove areas.

Fishing is an important activity and although most of the fish is consumed locally, small quantities of both fresh and frozen fish are exported. Fishermen are encouraged to purchase small powered craft and nylon nets by means of a Government hire purchase scheme and are instructed by the Fisheries Officer in the use and maintenance of modern types of gear.

The only industries are the distillation of clove-oil, coconut-oil expression, the manufacture of soap and the processing of coir fibre. The manufacture of coir yarn and coir yarn bags has recently commenced on a small scale.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The Clove Growers' Association, established in 1928 and incorporated by Decree in 1934, assists in stabilizing the price of cloves and distils and markets clove oil. MISCELLANEOUS ISLANDS

Various islands and rocks throughout the world are British territory or under British protection but are not included in any colony or separate protectorate. Many of these have no permanent inhabitants.
THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES (BASUTOLAND, THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE AND SWAZILAND)

Basutoland (a Colony), the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland (also a Protectorate), are administered on behalf of Her Majesty's Government in the United Kingdom by a High Commissioner. Each territory is administered on his behalf by a Resident Commissioner, responsible to the High Commissioner, and is divided into administrative districts in charge of District Commissioners. Since the advent of the South African Republic in May 1961 the High Commissioner has also held the office of British Ambassador to South Africa.

BASUTOLAND
Area

Comprises an area of 11,716 square miles of which about one quarter in the west is lowland country varying in height above sea level from 5,000 to 6,000 feet, the remaining three quarters being highlands rising to a height of 11,425 feet in the Drakensberg Range, which forms the eastern boundary with Natal.
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Population
In 1956 (the date of the last census) the population figures for Maseru township were: Africans 5,739, Europeans 590, Mixed Race 57 and one Asian.

A census of all races held in April 1956 gave the following figures for the population present in the Territory: Africans 638,857; Europeans 1,926; Persons of Mixed Race 644; and Asians 247. The census also gave the additional figures of 154,782 as an estimate of the number of Africans temporarily absent from the Colony for work and other purposes. The estimated total population of Basutoland in July 1961 was 697,000 with an additional 130,000 absent from the Territory. (By way of comparison, a census of 1875 gave a total of 469 Europeans and 127,707 Africans in Basutoland.)
Principal Towns
The capital is Maseru, which has an estimated population of about 10,000. The other principal towns are: Butha Buthe, Leribe, Mafeteng, Mokhotlong and Qachas Nek.
Geographical Features

The Colony of Basutoland is an enclave within the Republic of South Africa, its boundaries running with those of Natal to the east, the Cape Province to the south and the Orange Free State to the north and west. It lies between latitudes $28^\circ 35'$ and $30^\circ 40'$ south and longitudes $27^\circ 00'$ and $29^\circ 30'$ east. The mountain ranges run from north to south and those in the central area, named the Maluti, are spurs of the main Drakensberg which they join in the north, forming a high plateau varying in height from 9,000 to 10,500 feet. In this area two of the largest rivers in the Republic, the Orange and the Tugela, and tributaries of the Caledon, have their source.
Climate

Rainfall is variable and averages approximately 29 inches a year over the greater part of the country. Most of it falls between October and April, but there is normally no month which has less than half an inch. Unfortunately, river discharge statistics show that most of this water is lost to Basutoland in the form of run-off. The deciding factors from an agricultural point of view are whether the rain comes in steady soaking showers at intervals suited to the growth of the staple crops or whether it comes in the form of short and heavy storms, running to waste and eroding the soil. Serious droughts, like the one of 1948-49 are rare and there are a large number of perennial streams, though not so many as existed some years ago. In the lowlands the temperatures vary from a maximum of $90^\circ$ or more in summer to a minimum that rarely drops below $20^\circ$ in winter: in the highlands the range is much wider and temperatures below zero are common.
History

The Basotho as a nation did not come into prominence until 1818 when Moshesh, a minor chief of the Bakoena tribe in Northern Basutoland, gathered together the remnants of the various clans that had been scattered by the raids of the Zulu and Matebele.
The new nation went through a period of struggle, first with the emigrant Boers of the Great Trek, then with the British. From 1856 until 1868 there was a series of wars with the
Orange Free State burghers, Moshesh lost a large portion of his country and repeatedly sought the protection of the British Government.
In 1868 the Basotho became British subjects and the boundaries of the Territory were defined by the Cape of Good Hope Notice of 13th May 1870.

The country remained in a very unsettled condition until it was annexed to the Cape Colony in 1871. The people never took kindly to the Cape Colonial rule and in 1880, after various disturbances, the Governor of the Cape extended to Basutoland the provisions of the Cape Peace Preservation Act of 1878, which included a clause for the general disarmament of the Basotho. They refused to accept the terms, and, after a war lasting nearly a year, the Act was repealed, several chiefs would not submit, and it was decided to hand over the administration to the Imperial Government. This was effected by Order in Council of 2nd February 1884.
Constitution

Before the introduction of constitutional reforms in March 1960 Basutoland was governed under a loose-knit system by which the British Administration combined with an hereditary Chieftainship. The Basotho owe allegiance to a single Paramount Chief under whom there is a hierarchy of Chiefs and Headmen. These Chiefs and Headmen are the authorities with whom the ordinary people deal in their daily affairs, and are responsible for the day to day administration of the Territory.
The Head of the Administration is the Resident Commissioner, who works under the direction of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland.

In 1910 an advisory body known as the Basutoland Council consisting of the Resident Commissioner as President, the Paramount Chief as Chief Councillor and 99 Basotho nominated members, was constituted by Proclamation. The Council evolved gradually and an elected element, 42 in number, was introduced in 1957.
In 1943 District Councils, consisting in the main of nominated members and a small number of elected members, were established as advisory and consultative bodies.

In September 1955 the Basutoland National Council passed a motion requesting "that the Basutoland Council be given power to make laws in all internal matters, such laws to be confirmed by the Paramount Chief". In May 1956 the Secretary of State invited the Council to submit detailed recommendations for an advance in constitutional status. Agreement on a new constitution was reached at the end of 1958 and this was brought into operation in March 1960.
The new constitution provides for an Executive Council, which is advisory to both the High Commissioner and the Paramount Chief, and a Legislative Council known as the Basutoland National Council.

The Executive Council consists of the Resident Commissioner, who acts as Chairman, the Government Secretary, Finance Secretary, Assistant Attorney-General, three persons chosen by the Basutoland National Council from among their own number and one person nominated by the Paramount Chief.
The Basutoland National Council consists of 80 members, divided equally between elected and non-elected members; the former being elected
Digitized by
THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES

from among the members of District Councils by the District Councils sitting as electoral colleges for that purpose. The non-elected element is made up of the three official members of the Executive Council (excluding the Chairman), the Commissioner for Local Government, the 22 Principal and Ward Chiefs and 14 nominees of the Paramount Chief. The Council has power to legislate for all persons in Basutoland in respect of all matters, save the following, which are the High Commissioner's matters:

External Affairs and Defence; Internal Security; Currency, Public Loans, Customs and Excise; Copyright, Patents, Trade Marks and Designs; Posts (including Post Office Savings Bank), Telegrams, Telephones, Broadcasting and Television; the recruitment, appointment, conditions of service, promotion, discipline and retirement (including pensions) of officers in the Public Service.
In respect of legislation within the High Commissioner's field, the Basutoland National Council will continue to act as a consultative body and all matters will be referred to it.
National elections under the new constitution were held in January 1960 to elect the 162 members of the nine District Councils. The results were:—

Taxation
The main forms of direct taxation are Basuto tax, income tax and poll tax (payable by all male adult persons who do not pay Basuto tax).

The principal sources of indirect revenue are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Basutoland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades and businesses. There are also death duties, stamp duty and a wool and mohair export duty.
Public Finance
Excluding Colonial Development and Welfare funds.
Revenue is derived mainly under the following headings for which estimated receipts are given for 1961–62:—

£
Customs and Excise1,020,000
Taxes and duties471,000
Posts and telegraphs84,220
As revenue was expected to be substantially below expenditure and the Territory had no central balance the United Kingdom Government approved grant-in-aid of £1,155,000 for 1961–62.
Currency

The currency is the same as in the Republic of South Africa. During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from Sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 Sterling = R.2; 1 shilling = 10 cents).
Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act 1959 Basutoland received an allocation of £1.2m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959–64. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:—

£
Soil conservation and agriculture400,000
Medical services143,000
Education119,000
Roads and airfields104,000
Water and electricity supplies95,000
A further allocation of £418,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This will be used principally towards Education, Medical, Agriculture and Veterinary services.
Education

African education is largely in the hands of three missions (Paris Evangelical, Roman Catholic and Anglican), under the direction of the Education Department. Grants-in-aid are paid by Government to the three missions to cover the salaries of all teachers on the approved grant-in-aid list. In many aided schools of the Roman Catholic mission, assistance is given by unaided teachers, particularly Sisters. Small grants are paid from territorial funds to improve the equipment in primary schools. The total expenditure on grants-in-aid during 1960 was £290,099.

There are 1,110 schools and institutions in the Territory, of which all but 14 are under mission control; and of these mission schools 942 are either wholly or partly aided. The total enrolment in primary schools is 136,111. The average number of pupils per teacher is 54. In secondary schools the enrolment is 1,836; in teacher training schools 536; in technical schools 188; and in housecraft or craft schools 506.

The only institution providing post-secondary education in the Territory is the Pope Pius XII College at Roma, which is aided by a small Government grant. In 1960 it had an enrolment of 131 male and 36 female students. The college provides courses leading to the B.A. and B.Sc. and B.Comm. degrees and to the University Education Diploma of the University of South Africa.

All primary education is free and bursaries are provided for all stages of secondary, teacher training and University work. One Mosuto was studying at the University College of Fort Hare in 1960. Six bursars, including one woman, were undergoing medical training at the Durban Medical School. Twenty Basotho were studying at Universities and other institutions in the United Kingdom. One was studying in Southern Rhodesia, three at American universities and three in India. An increasing number of Basotho are being appointed to responsible posts in the Education Department; three Education Officers, three Assistant Education Officers and a Female Education Officer are now Basotho.
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Health

The Government Medical staff of the Territory consists of a Director of Health, 20 Medical Officers, one Medical Officer of Health and one Assistant Medical Officer, one Medical Officer for Mental Health and one Surgical Specialist. There are nine Government hospitals, staffed by European Nursing Sisters with subordinate African staff. There is accommodation for 587 patients in the Government hospitals. A 200 bed Government hospital in Maseru, which with the gracious consent of Her Majesty has been named the Queen Elizabeth II Hospital, was officially opened on the 25th July 1957. There are four mission hospitals subsidized by the Government, with a total accommodation of 338 beds. Health centres in mountain dispensaries provide out-patient medical facilities, and, where a trained African nurse is a member of the staff, maternity services to people living in remote areas. During 1960 17,795 patients were admitted to Government hospitals and 5,351 to mission hospitals. During the period 1937 to 1960 the outpatients at both Government and Mission dispensaries and clinics increased from 110,807 to 604,490. The Leper Settlement, four miles out of Maseru, had 318 patients at the end of 1960.

The principal diseases are venereal disease, chronic rheumatism, malnutrition, infections of the respiratory tract and dyspepsia. The heaviest toll of lives in children is due to tuberculosis, malnutrition, diphtheria, whooping-cough and gastro-enteritis. The incidence of nutritional and deficiency diseases is comparatively high and is allied to maize being the staple food. Typhus, plague and smallpox occur only rarely, and then in sporadic form, while diphtheria, typhoid fever, measles and whooping cough are endemic.
Communications

The Territory is linked with the railway system of the Republic of South Africa by a short branch-line from Maseru, the capital, to Marseilles on the Bloemfontein-Natal main line. One mile of this line is in Basutoland. The South African Railway runs near the boundaries of the Territory and goods are transported by road to the nearest railway station across the border.

The Road Motor Transport Department of the South African Railways runs regular services between Fouriesburg and Butha-Buthe, Zastron and Quthing, Zastron and Mohale's Hoek, and Quthing and Sebapala, and within the Territory in the lowland areas there are a number of bus services owned and operated by Basotho.

The greater part of Basutoland is very mountainous and in this area all transport is by pack animals. A new mountain motor road running eastwards from Maseru into the mountains has been constructed in order to open up the interior and facilitate its development and administration. The road has reached a point 80 miles from Maseru.

On the western side of Basutoland a fairly good road system has been developed in the strip of agricultural country running from north to south. The main roads traverse the whole of this area, with branches leading west to the principal points of exit. From the main roads a number of feeder-roads lead towards the foothills.
Principal Occupations
Basutoland is a pastoral country without factories or industrial undertakings, and since the

produce of the land is insufficient to support the total population, many able-bodied Basotho leave the Territory each year to take up employment in the Republic of South Africa. The Government protects labourers by forbidding recruitment for any mine or other concern where the conditions of work are not good. All labourers recruited by agents in Basutoland for work outside the Territory have to be attested before an official and the contract must be according to Government Regulations.
Main Crops and Products

So far there has been little evidence of any exploitable mineral deposits in the Territory, but recent prospecting for diamonds has shown encouraging results. For the present, agriculture and animal husbandry are Basutoland's main sources of wealth. The chief exports at present are wheat, wool and mohair. It is estimated that 1,000,000 acres are under cultivation.

The soils in the mountain areas are of basaltic origin, while those in the lowlands are derived mainly from underlying cave sandstone. In the lowlands the soil has been cropped continuously for upwards of 80 years. Due to the absence of fuel, practically all cattle manure is burnt, so that little or no organic matter is returned to the land. Thus with increasing population, both human and livestock, excessive demands have been made on the soil, which has lost its structure and become subject to serious erosion. The soils in the mountains which have been brought into cultivation comparatively recently are rich though shallow. Controlled grazing has brought back a grass cover and has effectively halted the loss of soil by storm water run off in the mountain areas. In 1960 some 1,249,000 trees were planted as part of soil conservation measures.
In 1960 there were 331,200 head of cattle and 1,037,300 sheep in the Territory. Wool, followed by mohair, hides and skins, continue to be the main source of cash income from the animal population.
Trade

Basutoland has no industries and imports the consumer goods and capital items it needs as well as a certain amount of agricultural produce and livestock. Exports consist almost entirely of agricultural commodities and livestock, the main exports being wool and mohair, followed by wheat, sorghum and cattle. The value of imports usually exceeds the value of exports but the adverse balance is partly offset by the export of labour to the Republic of South Africa.

THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE
Area

The Protectorate has not been surveyed as a whole, but its area is estimated at 225,000 square miles. The headquarters of the Protectorate Administration is at Mafeking, across the border in the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa.
Population
The last census was taken in 1956 and showed a population of 3,177 Europeans, 680 Coloured, 249 Asians and about 300,000 Africans.

The population is not evenly distributed, the bulk of the people living in the eastern part of the Protectorate with one fairly large tribe occupying the north-western corner. The central, western and southern areas of the territory, consisting for the most part of the Kalahari Desert, are extremely sparsely inhabited.
The Bechuana tribes tend to live in large villages or towns. About one-half of the population lives in towns of more than 1,000 inhabitants.
Principal Towns
Francistown, Gaberones, Kanye, Lobatsi, Maun and Serowe.
Geographical Features

The Bechuanaland Protectorate is bounded on the south by the Province of the Cape of Good Hope, on the south-east by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Southern Rhodesia, on the north by the Caprivi Zipfel and on the west by South West Africa. It lies between latitudes $18^{\circ}$ and $37^{\circ}$ south and longitudes $20^{\circ}$ and $29^{\circ} 50'$ east. The Kalahari Desert extends over most of the Protectorate west and south of Ngamiland as far as latitude $27^{\circ}$S. This is not a desert in the commonly accepted sense of the word, but consists of a vast expanse of undulating sand-belts with outcrops of limestone. In these limestone belts water is sometimes found at depths from 30 to 100 feet and it is possible that under the deep overburden of sand there may exist untapped reservoirs. The northern and south-western corners of the Kalahari have the least vegetation and most sand dunes, but elsewhere the grasses are excellent, and this is the natural habitat of the
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
aboriginal Bushmen. Large areas are wooded like parklands with camel-thorn and other indigenous trees.

In the north-western part of the Protectorate the Okovango River, flowing inland from Angola, widens into a delta from which flood-waters, in seasons of heavy rainfall, flow as far south-east as the Makarikari salt-lake and south and south-west into Lake Ngami. The Chobe River, also flowing from Angola, inundates a large area in the north before flowing into the Zambesi 60 miles west of the Victoria Falls.
The mean altitude of the Protectorate is 3,000 feet and in the eastern portion occasional elevations, e.g. at Kanye, Serowe and Ghanzi, reach 4,000 to 5,000 feet.
The Protectorate as a whole is a natural game-reserve for most species of the fauna of Africa and Government policy aims at their preservation.
Climate
The climate is sub-tropical (latitude 22°S. passes through the centre of the Protectorate) but varies with latitude and altitude.
The average annual rainfall is 18 inches, varying from 25 inches in the north to 9 inches or less in the western Kalahari.

In the higher parts, days are pleasantly warm and the nights cold during the winter, but the summer is universally hot. In August seasonal winds blow from the west coast and, desiccated by the Kalahari, carry sand and dust across the whole country.
The atmosphere throughout the year is very dry and this helps somewhat to mitigate the high temperature.
History

In 1884 the British Government appointed a Deputy Commissioner to the Bechuana tribes and in 1885 the whole of the Bechuanaland was, with the concurrence of Khama and other principal chiefs, proclaimed to be under the protection of Her Majesty The Queen. The southern part of the territory, which included Mafeking, Vryburg and Kuruman, was at the same time constituted a Crown Colony with the title British Bechuanaland. In 1885 British Bechuanaland was annexed to Cape Colony, subsequently the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa. The northern part, known as the Bechuanaland Protectorate, has continued to be administered by the Government of the United Kingdom.

The administration of the Bechuanaland Protectorate is governed by various Orders in Council and Proclamations, of which the most important is the Order in Council of Her Majesty Queen Victoria dated 9th May 1891. That Order empowered the then High Commissioner for South Africa to exercise on Her Majesty's behalf all the powers and jurisdiction which Her Majesty had or might have within the Protectorate and to that end authorized him to take or cause to be taken all such measures as he might think expedient, subject to such instructions as he might from time to time receive from Her Majesty or through a Secretary of State. Other provisions of the Order empowered the High Commissioner to appoint administrative and judicial officers and to assign their functions to them subject to the preservation of his own powers in their entirety and to provide, by proclamation from time to time, for the administration of justice, the raising
of revenue and the peace, order and good government of all persons within the Protectorate, including the prohibition and punishment of acts tending to disturb the public peace.

In issuing these proclamations the High Commissioner was instructed by the Order in Council to respect any Native laws and customs by which the civil relations of any Native chiefs, tribes or population under Her Majesty's protection were at that time (viz., in May 1891) regulated, except in so far as the same might be incompatible with the due exercise of Her Majesty's power and jurisdiction or which were repugnant to humanity.

The Order required the High Commissioner to publish his proclamations in the Gazette and reserved to Her Majesty the right to disallow any such proclamation. The Order also provided that, subject to any proclamation lawfully issued by the High Commissioner, any jurisdiction exercisable otherwise than under this Order in Council of 1891, whether by virtue of any Statute or Order in Council, or of any treaty, or otherwise, should remain in full force.
Her Majesty reserved the power to revoke, alter, add to or amend this Order at any time.
Constitution

The Protectorate is administered by a Resident Commissioner under the direction of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland and is divided for administrative purposes into ten districts under District Commissioners who are assisted in the maintenance of law and order by a force of police.
The territory comprises Crown lands, blocks of European farms and tribal Reserves.

In the African areas the method of administration is that generally known as "indirect rule". Native administrations were formally established and the powers and rights of Native authorities were defined in the Native Administration and Native Courts Proclamations of 1943 (Caps. 56 and 5 of the Laws, Cap. 56 having now been replaced by Proc. No. 24 of 1956) which replace the original Proclamations 74 and 75 of 1934. In 1938 Native Treasuries were successfully established in all Reserves. Government pays 50% of each Native Administration's collection of native tax to the tribal treasury concerned; other sources of revenue are a graded tax and standrents. The Chiefs and Finance Committees, under the guidance of District Commissioners, are responsible for the preparation of estimates and general management of their treasuries.

On 28th July 1960 the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations announced his approval of proposals for a new constitution in the Protectorate. These proposals were promulgated by the Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) Order in Council, 1960, and provide for a Legislative Council with power to legislate for the Protectorate, subject to certain reserve powers remaining with the High Commissioner. The Legislative Council has an elected unofficial majority; there are 21 elected members, of whom 10 are European, 10 African and one Asian; there are also 10 Government officials and four nominated unofficial members. The European elected members are elected by European voters, subject to certain qualifications of age, residence and property or income; the Asian elected member is elected by Asian voters similarly qualified. The African elected members are elected by an African Council consisting of Chiefs
THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES

of the eight principal tribes, 32 members appointed by tribes assembled in Kgotla or elected by the tribal or district councils where these exist, together with the official and appointed members. The Executive Council consists of six officials and four unofficials, two of whom are European and two African.
Taxation

The main forms of direct taxation are native tax, income tax, non-native poll tax and cattle export tax. The principal sources of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. The Bechuanaland Protectorate is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on all imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades, businesses and professions. There are also death duties.
Excluding Colonial Development and Welfare Funds.
Revenue is derived mainly under the following headings for which estimated receipts are given for 1961-62:

Customs and Excise .. .. .. £ 346,000
Taxes and duties .. .. .. 490,150
Posts and telegraphs .. .. .. 223,020
Revenue from Government property .. .. .. 182,050
As revenue was expected to be substantially below expenditure and the Territory had no central balance the United Kingdom Government approved a grant-in-aid of £1,157,000 for 1961-62.
Currency

During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 sterling = R. 2; 1 shilling = 10 cents). The Standard Bank of South Africa and Barclays Bank (D.C.O.) each have branches at Francistown, Lobatsi and Mahalapye.
Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act 1959 Bechuanaland received an allocation of £1.5m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959-64. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:

Agriculture and veterinary .. .. .. £ 350,000
Water supplies .. .. .. 334,000
Public building .. .. .. 416,000
Education .. .. .. 208,000
Roads .. .. .. 137,650
A further allocation of £165,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This will be used principally towards Education, Public Works, Agriculture and Veterinary.
Education

There are eight main tribes in the Protectorate. Communications are primitive, distances great and financial resources far from commensurate with urgent needs. Consequently the administration and promotion of African education are unusually difficult.

Most of the administrative work has devolved upon eight tribal central school committees in addition to which there are four committees for non-tribal polyglot areas and in three remote areas education is administered directly by the District Commissioners concerned and the Department of Education.

Primary Education. Enrolled in the 179 African primary schools in the Protectorate during 1959 were 32,406 pupils of whom approximately 60% were girls. The disparity between the sexes, caused by the absence of boys at cattle-posts and on the land, has had a retarding influence ever since schools were first established.

Post-Primary Education. The Bamangwato College was re-constituted as a territorial institution under a Governing Council by Proclamation No. 26 of 1956. All tribes are represented on the Council. A Colonial Development and Welfare grant of £30,000 was made available for the building of a classroom block and this has now been completed. The Government now meets recurrent costs. A secondary boarding school is conducted by the Roman Catholic Mission at Khale, while junior secondary day schools exist at Kanye, Mochudi and Molepolole. The total secondary enrolment in 1959 was 431.

Teacher Training. A new teacher training college was completed and occupied in July 1956. Classroom accommodation was extended in 1958. This institution can accommodate at least 100 students, twice the number previously in training. Cost of the buildings, met from Colonial Development and Welfare funds, was over £45,000.

Youth Movements. There are 3,348 Boy Scouts, divided into 58 groups and 4,523 Girl Guides, in 80 groups. Monthly bulletins are issued to all Scout and Guide officers. A Government grant of £200 per year, divided equally between the two movements, is applied chiefly to training and travelling expenses.

For the small European community nine primary schools are conducted. At Ghanzi, the most remote European settlement in Southern Africa, a boarding school capable of accommodating 60 pupils was opened in 1953. Two other hostels were erected for boarders at Francistown and Lobatsi in 1957 and opened in 1958. The number of European pupils enrolled in Protectorate primary schools was 451 in 1959.

In view of the small school community which it would serve, there is no provision for secondary education for European children in the Territory; the need is partially met by the award of bursaries and grants to enable children to attend schools in neighbouring territories.

The total expenditure on educational service from funds controlled by Government by Native Administrations and by missions during the year 1959 was £324,806. Of this total £89,347 was contributed by Colonial Development and Welfare schemes.
Health
There are four Government hospitals and four Government Health Centres, together with 36
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

dispensaries, eight of which are staffed by resident dispensers. Various missions administer two hospitals and one Health Centre and 29 out-station dispensaries. These hospitals, health centres and dispensaries provide 854 beds, equivalent to one bed for approximately 348 of the population. During 1959 16,967 patients were admitted to hospitals and attendances for treatment as out-patients numbered 316,797, plus 157,183 attendances for vaccinations, inoculations, etc., making a total of 473,980. Maternity work, including ante-natal and post-natal care, is mainly carried out in the hospitals but there is a special maternity centre administered by the London Missionary Society and supervised by the Government Medical Officer of the District.

The technical staff consists of the Director of Medical Services, Medical Officer of Health, one Senior Medical Officer, 13 Medical Officers, three Health Inspectors, one Matron, four Sisters-in-Charge, one Sister Tutor and 11 European Sisters. There is a subsidiary African staff, trained, semi-trained and untrained. In addition there are six medical missionaries and subsidiary staff, both European and African.

African nurses are trained at both Government and Mission hospitals. Midwives are trained at the Government Hospital, Serowe, at the Maun Maternity Centre and the Molopolole Mission. Nurses undergo a three-year course in general nursing, at the end of which they receive a year's training in midwifery.
African Sanitary Inspectors are trained under the supervision of the Health Inspectors.
Communications

The main railway line from Cape Town to the north runs through the Protectorate, entering it at Ramathlabama, 886 miles from Cape Town, and leaving it at Ramaquabane, 398 miles farther north. The line runs roughly parallel to the eastern boundary of the territory at an average distance from it of about 55 miles. The railway within the territory is owned by the Rhodesia Railways and the section Mahalapye/Ramathlabama is operated by the South African Railways Administration as agents. The gauge is 3 feet 6 inches.

The roads are almost exclusively of earth or sand, the only exceptions being short lengths of gravel, and, in two townships, bitumen surfaces over short distances. There is limited bridging over the main rivers.
Twenty-four landing-grounds and emergency strips are maintained in the Protectorate.
Principal Occupations

Over 95% of the population is engaged in stock raising. A few Africans are employed as drovers and herdsmen by European farmers but the great majority are themselves owners of livestock, which are cared for on a family or tribal basis. The principal occupations of the small number of African wage earners are:

Government service 2,500*
Agriculture 3,000
Building 300
Trade and industry 2,000
Domestic service 2,000
* This figure includes approximately 1,000 casual labourers.
Main Crops and Products

The main occupation of the Bechuana is cattle raising. The number of cattle in 1959 was 1,317,236 of which 10,196 animals and 42,206,082 lb. of carcases were exported. There were also 343,665 sheep, goats and pigs. Seasonal lack of water and grazing and indifferent animal husbandry are the chief handicaps to the expansion of the industry.

Irregular rainfall and primitive agricultural practices, aggravated by the migration of able-bodied men to work in the Republic are the cause of occasional crop failures. The principal crops are sorghum and beans.
A little gold and some kyanite are mined in the north-east, asbestos near Kanye in the Banwaketse Reserve and manganese in the Bangwaketse and Bamalete Reserves.

SWAZILAND
Area

Swaziland Protectorate, the smallest of the three High Commission territories is bordered on the north, west and south by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Mocambique and on the south-east by Natal. It lies between latitudes $24^{\circ} 50'$ and $26^{\circ} 40'$ south and longitudes $30^{\circ} 25'$ and $32^{\circ} 5'$ east. With a total area of 6,704 square miles, it is a country of many diversities and many distinctive characteristics.
Population

The 1956 Census showed that the population of Swaziland was 240,511. This figure included 11,728 Swazi who were temporarily employed outside the Territory and 3,470 foreign Africans temporarily employed in Swaziland. Africans comprised 97% of the population, Europeans 2.4% and Euroafricans 0.6%. At the end of 1960 the population was estimated at 273,000 including approximately 10,500 Swazi temporarily absent. This figure is broken down as follows; 261,500 Swazis, 9,700 Europeans, 1,800 Euroafricans.
Principal Towns
The capital is Mbabane and other principal towns are: Hlatikulu, Mankaiana, Manzine, Piggs Peak and Stegi.
Geographical Features

Geographically and climatically, it is divisible into four physiographic provinces—the broken mountainous high-veld in the west, with altitudes averaging over 4,000 feet, the middle-veld which is mostly mixed farming country about 2,000 feet lower and the low-veld, a hot woodland region typified by aloes, acacias and pterocarpus, and bounded on the east by the Lubombo mountains, with an average altitude of 1,500 feet. The Lubombo mountains form the fourth physiographic province. Four rivers, the Komati, Usutu, Mbuluzi and Ngwavuma, flow from west to east, cutting their way through the Lubombo mountains to the Indian Ocean. The exploitation of these rivers is particularly important to the agricultural development of the middle and bush veld, where irrigation projects are giving the scenery a different aspect.
Climate

75% to 80% of the rains come in the summer half of the year from October to March. Year to year variability of totals is extremely large, especially in the low-veld. Drought hazard is also most serious in the low-veld. The average rainfall

at Mbabane in the high-veld is 54.6 inches, at Manzini (formerly Bremersdorp) in the middle-veld 35.9 inches and at Sipofaneni in the low-veld 26.5 inches. The mean maximum and minimum temperatures are respectively $72.6^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and $51.7^{\circ}\text{F.}$ at Mbabane; $79.7^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and $56.4^{\circ}\text{F.}$ at Manzini; and $84.4^{\circ}\text{F.}$ and $59.5^{\circ}\text{F.}$ at Sipofaneni.
History

Swaziland remained nominally independent until 1890. From 1890 to 1894 the United Kingdom and the Transvaal (South African Republic) Governments established a species of condominium which was replaced in 1894 by an arrangement under which the South African Republic was given powers of protection and administration without actual incorporation. After the Anglo-Boer war, the administration of Swaziland was transferred to the Governor of the Transvaal, who was also the High Commissioner of South Africa, but in 1907 responsibility for Swaziland was transferred from the Governor to the High Commissioner for South Africa. Swaziland therefore remained under the control of the United Kingdom when the Transvaal became a province of the Union of South Africa in 1910.
Constitution

In 1902, at the end of the Anglo-Boer war, the British Special Commissioner with a small force of the South African Constabulary was sent into Swaziland to establish a provisional Administration. Shortly afterwards, an Order in Council dated the 25th of June 1903, was issued which established the relationship between Great Britain and the Swazi and constitutes the basic authority under which the Administration has been conducted by Her Majesty's Government.

At the head of Government is the Resident Commissioner, who is subject to the direction and instructions of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland. The Resident Commissioner exercises such administration and control and is vested with all such powers, authorities and jurisdiction as are conferred upon him by the Swaziland Administration Proclamation of 1904 and other laws, or by the terms of his Commission. He is assisted and advised by the Government Secretary, who is also the Deputy Resident Commissioner, and by the heads of the various Government departments. The Territory is divided into six districts which are administered by District Commissioners stationed at Mbabane, Manzini, Hlatikulu, Piggs Peak, Mankaiana and Stegi. The District Commissioners are the principal executive officers of Government within their districts and directly responsible to the Resident Commissioner for their efficient administration. The Judiciary falls under the Chief Justice of the High Commission Territories.

The Resident Commissioner is advised by the European Advisory Council on matters directly affecting the European residents of Swaziland and on any other matters specifically referred to the Council by the Resident Commissioner. The Territory is divided into ten electoral divisions and each returns one member to the Council, which usually meets twice a year. Urgent matters are, however, dealt with by the Council's Standing Committee. On matters concerning the African population the Resident Commissioner consults the Paramount Chief and through him the Swazi National Council.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
The Constitutional Committee set up in 1960 has completed its work and presented its report to the Secretary of State.

A great deal of the structure of the traditional Swazi political system has been retained in the modern pattern of the Swazi National Administration. The Ngwenyama (the Paramount Chief) is the constitutional ruler who is advised by his kinsmen and his chosen Councillors and cannot initiate action without the approval of two formally constituted Councils. The Liqoqo, comprising the more important of Ngwenyama's kinsmen and a number of chosen advisers, and the Libandla, which at its widest extension is a Council of every adult male in the nation. It is recognised as the final body from which approval for any contemplated act or legislation should be obtained.
From the central institutions of the Swazi National Administration, responsibility for the routine administration of the country devolves upon the Chiefs and Indunas.

There are proclaimed townships at Mbabane, Manzini, Stegi, Hlatikulu and Goedegun. In each of these townships and in Piggs Peak village, an Urban Area Advisory Committee functions under the chairmanship of the District Commissioner and advises him on the administration and welfare of the township and surrounding urban area. These Committees consist of five elected members and Government officials nominated by the Resident Commissioner. There are, in addition, African Advisory Committees which advise the District Commissioner on the needs of the urban African population. Attention is now being given to the question of granting municipal status to the townships of Mbabane and Manzini. Whilst no formal machinery has been established to effect joint consultation between the different communities in the Territory, Standing Committees of the Swazi Nation and the European Advisory Council, as well as representatives of the Euroafrican Welfare Association, meet together to discuss matters of territorial importance. In terms of the Swaziland Administration Proclamation, 107, Roman Dutch Common Law is the common law of the Territory, except where modified by statute. In civil matters Swazi law and custom may be followed where necessary. Statute law consists of Transvaal laws in force at the date of the Administration Proclamation and all subsequent laws promulgated by the High Commissioner. A Special Court, now the High Court, having the full jurisdiction of a Superior Court, was also established together with Courts of District Commissioners with a limited jurisdiction. A Court of Appeal for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland was established in 1955, but the right of further appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council was retained.
Taxation

The main forms of direct taxation are African tax, European poll tax and income tax, towards which mining companies contribute the major share. The principal sources of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Swaziland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades. There are also death duties.
Public Finance
Excluding expenditure from Colonial Development and Welfare funds.
Revenue is derived mainly under the following headings for which estimated receipts are given for 1961-62:—

Customs and Excise .. .. £540,000
Income tax .. .. 1,562,000
Posts and telegraphs .. .. 315,000
As revenue was expected to fall below expenditure in 1961-62 and the central balance would be exhausted, the United Kingdom Government approved a grant-in-aid of £390,000.
Currency

The currency in circulation in Swaziland is that of the Republic of South Africa. During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from Sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 sterling = R.2; 1 shilling = 10 cents).
Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act, 1959, Swaziland received an allocation of a little over £4m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959-64. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:

Roads .. .. £920,000
Education .. .. 673,000
Water supplies .. .. 500,000
Agriculture .. .. 320,000
A further allocation of £280,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This will be used principally towards Education, Hospital extensions and Electricity supplies.

The construction of the first railway in Swaziland has recently been approved and the line should be open for traffic during 1964. The railway will run a distance of 140 miles to the Mozambique border and will eventually link Swaziland with the port of Lourenço Marques. Its construction will make possible the development of large iron ore deposits at Ngwenya and will facilitate the export of wood pulp and sugar.
Education

African Primary Education is largely the responsibility of a number of Church Missions which receive substantial financial assistance from the Government in the form of salary and equipment grants. Apart from 236 schools falling under the direct superintendence of the Missions there are 19 Government maintained schools, three National schools financed by the Swazi Treasury and 36 tribal schools. Enrolment which stood at 13,797 in 1950 reached 37,339 Swazis in 1961. In addition to the primary schools there are three high schools, 20 junior secondary schools,
THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES
two Teacher Training Centres, a Housecraft Training Centre and a Trades School which offers courses in building, carpentry and motor mechanics.

Many bursaries are granted each year to assist students taking secondary school courses leading to matriculation, teacher training courses, post-matriculation courses at University level and various forms of industrial training.

For European children, education is compulsory up to the age of sixteen or the successful completion of Standard VIII. There are two high schools and 11 primary schools with a total enrolment of 1,540 in 1961.

The Coloured, or Euroafrican, community is numerically the smallest group in Swaziland and comprises a wide range of types with varying material and cultural standards. The educational requirements of coloured children are met by five Government-aided Mission boarding-schools, one of which offers secondary education up to matriculation and two to the Junior Certificate. There is also a Housecraft Training Centre for girls, while coloured boys may and do enrol at the Mbabane Trades School. Total enrolment in 1961 was 682.
Health

The medical staff of the Administration consists of the Director of Medical Services, nine Medical Officers, three Health Inspectors, two Dispenser-Storekeepers, one Laboratory technician, two Matrons and 12 Nursing Sisters. Seven doctors and 13 mission nursing sisters are subsidised by the Government.

There are two Government general hospitals, one at Mbabane and the other at Hlatikulu. In the former there is accommodation for 151 patients and 135 in the latter. There is a 24-bed cottage hospital at Mankaiana and another with 16 beds at Piggs Peak. The Raleigh Fitkin Memorial (Mission) Hospital at Manzini provides accommodation for 200 patients, the Roman Catholic Mission Hospital at Stegi for 35 patients and the Mahamba Methodist Hospital for 73 patients.

There are 11 Government, 16 Mission and three other Clinics financed by the Swazi National Treasury, throughout the Territory. A Health Office containing a well equipped Public Health Laboratory is at Manzini.
Communications

The road motor-transport services of the South African Railways Administration and the Portuguese East African Administration carry both goods and passengers and operate between Swaziland and the railheads in the neighbouring territories. A railway line to the Mozambique border is under construction.
The Territory is served by a network of roads which are being rapidly brought to acceptable standards. There is an extensive telecommunications system.
Main Crops and Products

Cattle form the main wealth of the Swazis and the total number owned increased from 146,542 in 1921 to 434,995 in 1949; in 1960 the number was 401,973. The number of cattle owned by others increased from 63,748 in 1921 to 119,481 in 1960. In 1960 there were 204,214 goats, 35,482 sheep and 12,696 pigs.

Slaughter cattle are exported to markets in Johannesburg and Durban which are controlled by export quotas allocated by the South African Meat Industry Control Board. The numbers of cattle exported increased by 27% in 1960 compared with those of 1959. Sales of butter during 1960 amounted to close on 158,000 lbs., an increase of 22% on the 1959 figures.

Mining is an important part of Swaziland's economy. The Havelock asbestos mine in the north-western corner of the Territory is one of the largest asbestos mines in the world and is apparently assured of a life of at least 30 years at present rates of output. Rich deposits of iron ore exist north-west of Mbabane and will be developed as a result of the decision to build a railway link to Mozambique. There is a small tin-mining industry and barytes is being worked in the Mbabane district. There are very extensive anthracite coal deposits in the low-veld. High-grade calcite exists in the Hlatikulu District and a rich iron-ore deposit in the Piggs Peak area. Corundum and beryl have also been found.

The agricultural possibilities of Swaziland are considerable and most crops can be grown. In the past seven years, there has been an immense improvement in agricultural methods among the Swazi due to the stimulus of the rural development schemes which were inaugurated in 1949.

The most important dry-land crop is maize, but only a proportion of the Territory's home consumption is grown in Swaziland. The most important dry-land cash crops during 1960 were cotton, which was valued at R.434,595, tobacco, production of which amounted to 959,297 lbs. of leaf (resulting in a total first payment to growers of R.98,436) and pineapples. Some 1,550 tons of canned pineapples (valued at R.252,000) were exported to the United Kingdom during the year. Both the cotton and the tobacco were record crops. All the cotton grown in Swaziland is exported to the ginneries in the Republic of South Africa, while the tobacco is sold to a Tobacco Co-operative at Goedegun.

In 1957 the Colonial Development Corporation completed the construction of a gravity canal which is able to command up to 35,000 acres, 8,200 acres of which have already been planted out to sugar. This project on the Komati, together with a similar one on the Usutu River, each have a new sugar mill with annual quotas for the production of a total of 80,000 tons of sugar. There are several other minor irrigation schemes throughout the middle and low veld of the Territory.
By December 1960 there were over 300,000 citrus trees in orchards in the Territory. The figure to 1959 was 193,886.

Considerable developments have taken place in forestry in the last two decades. The total area set aside for afforestation is upwards of 200,000 acres and by the end of 1960 190,000 acres had already been afforested. There are three main projects with a total of 171,963 acres including 3,000 acres of the Swazi National forests which is managed by Usutu Forests Limited. The largest of the three projects, Usutu Forests with 97,500 acres of plantation, reached the production stage in 1961. The Usutu Pulp Company with a Share capital of R.10,000,000 was formed in 1959 to produce and export unbleached pulp. The pulp will be exported to United Kingdom markets via Lourenço Marques.
